**Pashtuns**[[g]](#cite_note-34) are an [Iranian ethnic group](/wiki/Iranian_peoples "Iranian peoples")[[24]](#cite_note-Minahan-29) primarily residing in southern and eastern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and northwestern [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[28]](#cite_note-Caldwell2011-35)[[29]](#cite_note-Brit-Pashtun-36) They were historically referred to as **[Afghans](/wiki/Afghan_(ethnonym) "Afghan (ethnonym)")**[[h]](#cite_note-42) even into the 1970s, although the constitution of 1923 began the use of 'Afghans' as a demonym for all citizens of the Kingdom,[[35]](#cite_note-43)[[36]](#cite_note-AO-1923-44)[[37]](#cite_note-Greenwood-45)[[38]](#cite_note-46) regardless of their [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Afghanistan "Ethnic groups in Afghanistan"), to create an Afghan national identity.[[37]](#cite_note-Greenwood-45)[[39]](#cite_note-47)

The Pashtuns speak the [Pashto language](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto"), which belongs to the [Eastern Iranian](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") branch of the [Iranian language family](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages"). The dialect of [Wanetsi](/wiki/Wanetsi "Wanetsi") is spoken mainly among Pashtuns of the [Tareen tribe](/wiki/Tareen "Tareen") in Pakistan, and [Ormuri](/wiki/Ormuri "Ormuri") among non-Pashtun [Ormur people](/wiki/Ormur "Ormur") and [Wazir Pashtuns](/wiki/Wazir_(Pashtun_tribe) "Wazir (Pashtun tribe)"). Additionally, [Dari](/wiki/Dari "Dari") serves as the second language of Pashtuns in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"),[[40]](#cite_note-48)[[41]](#cite_note-49) while those in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") speak [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language").[[22]](#cite_note-2012Hakala-27)[[42]](#cite_note-50) In [India](/wiki/India "India"), the majority of those of Pashtun descent have lost the ability to speak Pashto and instead speak [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [other regional languages](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"),[[43]](#cite_note-51)[[23]](#cite_note-Green2017-28)[[44]](#cite_note-Krishnamurthy2013-52) while those in Iran primarily speak [Southern Pashto](/wiki/Southern_Pashto "Southern Pashto"), and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") as a second language.

Pashtuns form the world's largest [tribal](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") [society](/wiki/Social_group "Social group"), comprising from 60–70 million people, and between 350–400 [tribes](/wiki/Pashtun_tribes "Pashtun tribes"), plus more sub-tribes. There are a [variety of origin theories](/wiki/Theories_of_Pashtun_origin "Theories of Pashtun origin") for the peoples.[[45]](#cite_note-53)[[46]](#cite_note-Romano1-54)[[47]](#cite_note-Jamestown-55)[[48]](#cite_note-NatGeo-56)[[49]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-Pashto1993-57)[[46]](#cite_note-Romano1-54)[[47]](#cite_note-Jamestown-55)[[50]](#cite_note-ThoughtCo-58) In 2021, [Shahid Javed Burki](/wiki/Shahid_Javed_Burki "Shahid Javed Burki") estimated the total Pashtun population to be between 60 and 70 million, with 15 million in Afghanistan.[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) Others who accept the 15 million figure include British academic Tim Willasey-Wilsey[[2]](#cite_note-auto1-2) as well as Abubakar Siddique, a journalist specializing in Afghan affairs.[[3]](#cite_note-auto2-3) This figure is disputed because there has been no official census in Afghanistan since 1979 due to [ongoing conflicts](/wiki/Afghan_conflict "Afghan conflict") there.[[51]](#cite_note-59)

They are the largest [ethnic group in Afghanistan](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Afghanistan "Ethnic groups in Afghanistan") and the second-largest ethnic group in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"),[[4]](#cite_note-CIA-5) constituting around 42–47% of the total [Afghan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Afghanistan "Demographics of Afghanistan") [population](/wiki/Demographics_of_Afghanistan "Demographics of Afghanistan") and around 15.4% of the total [Pakistani population](/wiki/Demographics_of_Pakistan "Demographics of Pakistan")[[52]](#cite_note-60)[[53]](#cite_note-61)[[54]](#cite_note-62)[[4]](#cite_note-CIA-5) In India, significant and historical communities of the [Pashtun diaspora](/wiki/Pashtun_diaspora "Pashtun diaspora") exist in the northern region of [Rohilkhand](/wiki/Rohilkhand "Rohilkhand"), as well as in major Indian cities such as [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") and [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai").[[55]](#cite_note-CanfieldPaleczek-63)[[56]](#cite_note-The_Hindu-64)

### Afghanistan and Pakistan

See also: [Ethnic groups in Afghanistan](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Afghanistan "Ethnic groups in Afghanistan") and [Ethnic groups in Pakistan](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Pakistan "Ethnic groups in Pakistan")

Pashtuns are spread over a wide geographic area, south of the [Amu river](/wiki/Amu_river "Amu river") and west of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"). They can be found all over Afghanistan and Pakistan.[[28]](#cite_note-Caldwell2011-35) Big cities with a Pashtun majority include [Jalalabad](/wiki/Jalalabad "Jalalabad"), [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar"), [Bannu](/wiki/Bannu "Bannu"), [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan "Dera Ismail Khan"), [Khost](/wiki/Khost "Khost"), [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat "Kohat"), [Lashkar Gah](/wiki/Lashkar_Gah "Lashkar Gah"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan "Mardan"), [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni"), [Mingora](/wiki/Mingora "Mingora"), [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Quetta](/wiki/Quetta "Quetta"), among others. Pashtuns also live in [Abbottabad](/wiki/Abbottabad "Abbottabad"), [Farah](/wiki/Farah,_Afghanistan "Farah, Afghanistan"), [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat"), [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"), [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), [Kunduz](/wiki/Kunduz "Kunduz"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Mazar-i-Sharif](/wiki/Mazar-i-Sharif "Mazar-i-Sharif"), [Mianwali](/wiki/Mianwali "Mianwali"), and [Attock](/wiki/Attock "Attock").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city of [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), the financial capital of Pakistan, is home to the world's largest urban community of Pashtuns, larger than those of [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") and [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar").[[57]](#cite_note-65) Likewise, [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), the country's political capital, also serves as the major urban center of Pashtuns. More than 20% of the city's population belongs to the [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto")-speaking community.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### India

Main article: [Pathans in India](/wiki/Pathans_in_India "Pathans in India")

Further information: [Rohillas](/wiki/Rohillas "Rohillas"), [Pashtuns of Kashmir](/wiki/Pashtuns_of_Kashmir "Pashtuns of Kashmir"), [Pathans of Punjab](/wiki/Pathans_of_Punjab "Pathans of Punjab"), [Pathans of Sindh](/wiki/Pathans_of_Sindh "Pathans of Sindh"), [Pathans of Gujarat](/wiki/Pathans_of_Gujarat "Pathans of Gujarat"), [Pathans of Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Pathans_of_Uttar_Pradesh "Pathans of Uttar Pradesh"), and [Pathans of Bihar](/wiki/Pathans_of_Bihar "Pathans of Bihar")

Pashtuns in India often identify as *Pathans* (the Hindustani word for Pashtun), and are referred to this way by other ethnic groups of the subcontinent.[[i]](#cite_note-67)[[59]](#cite_note-Morton-Jack2015-68)[[60]](#cite_note-69)[[61]](#cite_note-70) Some Indians claim descent from Pashtun soldiers who settled in India by marrying local women during the [Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent").[[62]](#cite_note-Indian_Pathans-71)

Many Pathans chose to live in the Republic of India after the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"). Khan Mohammad Atif, a professor at the [University of Lucknow](/wiki/University_of_Lucknow "University of Lucknow"), estimates that "The population of Pathans in India is twice their population in Afghanistan".[[63]](#cite_note-Alavi2008-72)

Historically, Pashtuns settled in various cities of India before and during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in [colonial India](/wiki/Colonial_India "Colonial India"). These include Bombay (now called [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai")), [Farrukhabad](/wiki/Farrukhabad "Farrukhabad"), [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), [Saharanpur](/wiki/Saharanpur "Saharanpur"), [Rohilkhand](/wiki/Rohilkhand "Rohilkhand"), [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur "Jaipur"), and [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore").[[55]](#cite_note-CanfieldPaleczek-63)[[64]](#cite_note-Khyber-73)[[56]](#cite_note-The_Hindu-64) The settlers are descended from both Pashtuns of present-day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") ([British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") before 1947). In some regions in India, they are sometimes referred to as *Kabuliwala*.[[65]](#cite_note-74)

In India significant Pashtun diaspora communities exist.[[66]](#cite_note-India-census-75)[[62]](#cite_note-Indian_Pathans-71) While speakers of Pashto in the country number only 21,677 as of 2011, estimates of the ethnic or ancestral Pashtun population in India range from 3,200,000[[67]](#cite_note-Ali2018-76)[[68]](#cite_note-TNI2018-77)[[69]](#cite_note-Bhattacharya2018-78) to 11,482,000,[[70]](#cite_note-Joshua-79) to as high as double their population in Afghanistan (approximately 30 million).[[j]](#cite_note-Pathans-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Major_ethnic_groups_of_Pakistan_in_1980_borders_removed.jpg/250px-Major_ethnic_groups_of_Pakistan_in_1980_borders_removed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Major_ethnic_groups_of_Pakistan_in_1980_borders_removed.jpg)

Pashtun-inhabited areas in Pakistan and Afghanistan (including the southern borders of the former Soviet Union, the northeastern borders of Iran, and the northwestern borders of India disputed with Pakistan), in early 1980s.

The [Rohilkhand](/wiki/Rohilkhand "Rohilkhand") region of [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") is named after the [Rohilla](/wiki/Rohilla "Rohilla") community of Pashtun ancestry; the area came to be governed by the [Royal House of Rampur](/wiki/Rohilla_dynasty "Rohilla dynasty"), a [Pashtunized](/wiki/Pashtunized "Pashtunized") [Jat](/wiki/Jat_Muslim "Jat Muslim") dynasty.[[71]](#cite_note-Frey2020-81)[[72]](#cite_note-82) They also live in the states of [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra") in central India and [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") in eastern India that each have a population of over a million with Pashtun ancestry;[[70]](#cite_note-Joshua-79) both [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") and [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta") were primary locations of Pashtun migrants from [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") during the colonial era.[[73]](#cite_note-83) There are also populations over 100,000 each in the cities of [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur "Jaipur") in [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan") and [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore") in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka").[[70]](#cite_note-Joshua-79) Bombay (now called [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai")) and [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") both have a Pashtun population of over 1 million, while [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur "Jaipur") and [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore") have an estimate of around 100,000. The Pashtuns in [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore") include the Khan siblings [Feroz](/wiki/Feroz_Khan_(actor) "Feroz Khan (actor)"), [Sanjay](/wiki/Sanjay_Khan "Sanjay Khan") and [Akbar Khan](/wiki/Akbar_Khan_(director) "Akbar Khan (director)"), whose father settled in [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore") from [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni").[[74]](#cite_note-84)

During the 19th century, when the British were recruiting peasants from British India as indentured servants to work in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and other places, Rohillas were sent to [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), [Surinam](/wiki/Surinam_(Dutch_colony) "Surinam (Dutch colony)"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana"), and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), to work in the sugarcane fields and perform manual labour.[[75]](#cite_note-85) Many stayed and formed communities of their own. Some of them [assimilated](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") with the other South Asian Muslim nationalities to form a common Indian Muslim community in tandem with the larger Indian community, losing their distinctive heritage. Some Pashtuns travelled as far as [Australia](/wiki/Afghan_(Australia) "Afghan (Australia)") during the same era.[[76]](#cite_note-86)

Today, the Pashtuns are a collection of diversely scattered communities present across the length and breadth of India, with the largest populations principally settled in the plains of [northern](/wiki/North_India "North India") and [central India](/wiki/Central_India "Central India").[[77]](#cite_note-Khan2015-87)[[78]](#cite_note-Swarup-88)[[j]](#cite_note-Pathans-80) Following the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, many of them [migrated to Pakistan](/wiki/Muhajir_(Urdu-speaking_people) "Muhajir (Urdu-speaking people)").[[77]](#cite_note-Khan2015-87) The majority of Indian Pashtuns are [Urdu-speaking communities](/wiki/Urdu-speaking_people "Urdu-speaking people"),[[79]](#cite_note-Green20172-89) who have assimilated into the [local society](/wiki/Culture_of_India "Culture of India") over the course of generations.[[79]](#cite_note-Green20172-89) Pashtuns have influenced and contributed to various fields in India, particularly politics, the entertainment industry and sports.[[j]](#cite_note-Pathans-80)

### Iran

Pashtuns are also found in smaller numbers in the eastern and northern parts of [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran").[[80]](#cite_note-90) Records as early as the mid-1600s report [Durrani Pashtuns](/wiki/Durrani "Durrani") living in the [Khorasan province](/wiki/Khorasan_province "Khorasan province") of [Safavid Iran](/wiki/Safavid_Iran "Safavid Iran").[[81]](#cite_note-Dorrani-91) After the short reign of the [Ghilji Pashtuns in Iran](/wiki/Hotak_dynasty "Hotak dynasty"), [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah") defeated the last independent Ghilji ruler of [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar"), [Hussain Hotak](/wiki/Hussain_Hotak "Hussain Hotak"). In order to secure Durrani control in southern Afghanistan, Nader Shah deported [Hussain Hotak](/wiki/Hussain_Hotak "Hussain Hotak") and large numbers of the [Ghilji Pashtuns](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji") to the [Mazandaran province](/wiki/Mazandaran_province "Mazandaran province") in northern Iran. The remnants of this once sizeable exiled community, although assimilated, continue to claim Pashtun descent.[[82]](#cite_note-92) During the early 18th century, in the course of a very few years, the number of Durrani Pashtuns in Iranian Khorasan, greatly increased.[[k]](#cite_note-93) Later the region became part of the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") itself. The second Durrani king of Afghanistan, [Timur Shah Durrani](/wiki/Timur_Shah_Durrani "Timur Shah Durrani") was born in [Mashhad](/wiki/Mashhad "Mashhad").[[83]](#cite_note-94) Contemporary to Durrani rule in the east, [Azad Khan Afghan](/wiki/Azad_Khan_Afghan "Azad Khan Afghan"), an ethnic Ghilji Pashtun, formerly second in charge of [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") during [Afsharid rule](/wiki/Afsharid_dynasty "Afsharid dynasty"), gained power in the western regions of Iran and [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_(Iran) "Azerbaijan (Iran)") for a short period.[[84]](#cite_note-95) According to a sample survey in 1988, 75 per cent of all Afghan refugees in the [southern part of the Iranian Khorasan province](/wiki/South_Khorasan_province "South Khorasan province") were Durrani Pashtuns.[[l]](#cite_note-96)

### In other regions

Further information: [Afghan diaspora](/wiki/Afghan_diaspora "Afghan diaspora") and [Overseas Pakistani](/wiki/Overseas_Pakistani "Overseas Pakistani")

Indian and Pakistani Pashtuns have utilized the British/[Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") links of their respective countries, and modern communities have been established starting around the 1960s mainly in the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia but also in other [commonwealth countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_countries "Commonwealth countries") (and the United States). Some Pashtuns have also settled in the Middle East, such as in the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula"). For example, about 300,000 Pashtuns migrated to the [Persian Gulf countries](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf") between 1976 and 1981, representing 35% of Pakistani immigrants.[[85]](#cite_note-Jaffrelot-97) The [Pakistani](/wiki/Overseas_Pakistani "Overseas Pakistani") and [Afghan diaspora](/wiki/Afghan_diaspora "Afghan diaspora") around the world includes Pashtuns.

## Etymology

### Ancient historical references: Pashtun

A tribe called [Pakthās](/wiki/Pakthas "Pakthas"), one of the tribes that fought against [Sudas](/wiki/Sudas "Sudas") in the [Dasarajna](/wiki/Dasarajna "Dasarajna"), or *"Battle of the Ten Kings"*, are mentioned in [the seventh mandala](/wiki/Mandala_7 "Mandala 7") of the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda"), a text of [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") hymns dated between c. 1500 and 1200 BCE:[[86]](#cite_note-98)[[87]](#cite_note-99)

> Together came the Pakthas (पक्थास), the Bhalanas, the Alinas, the Sivas, the Visanins. Yet to the Trtsus came the Ārya's Comrade, through love of spoil and heroes' war, to lead them.

— Rigveda, Book 7, Hymn 18, Verse 7

[Heinrich Zimmer](/wiki/Heinrich_Zimmer "Heinrich Zimmer") connects them with a tribe mentioned by [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") (*Pactyans*) in 430 BCE in the [Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)"):[[88]](#cite_note-100)[[89]](#cite_note-101)[[90]](#cite_note-102)

> Other Indians dwell near the town of Caspatyrus[Κασπατύρῳ] and the Pactyic [Πακτυϊκῇ] country, north of the rest of India; these live like the Bactrians; they are of all Indians the most warlike, and it is they who are sent for the gold; for in these parts all is desolate because of the sand.

— Herodotus, The Histories, Book III, Chapter 102, Section 1

These Pactyans lived on the eastern frontier of the [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") [Arachosia](/wiki/Arachosia "Arachosia") [Satrapy](/wiki/Satrapy "Satrapy") as early as the 1st millennium BCE, present-day Afghanistan.[[91]](#cite_note-Heredotus-103) [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") also mentions a tribe of known as Aparytai (Ἀπαρύται).[[92]](#cite_note-104) [Thomas Holdich](/wiki/Thomas_Holdich "Thomas Holdich") has linked them with the [Afridi](/wiki/Afridi "Afridi") tribe:[[93]](#cite_note-105)[[94]](#cite_note-106)[[95]](#cite_note-107)

> The Sattagydae, Gandarii, Dadicae, and Aparytae (Ἀπαρύται) paid together a hundred and seventy talents; this was the seventh province

— Herodotus, The Histories, Book III, Chapter 91, Section 4

[Joseph Marquart](/wiki/Josef_Markwart "Josef Markwart") made the connection of the Pashtuns with names such as the Parsiētai (Παρσιῆται), Parsioi (Πάρσιοι) that were cited by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") 150 CE:[[96]](#cite_note-108)[[97]](#cite_note-109)

> "The northern regions of the country are inhabited
> by the Bolitai, the western regions by the Aristophyloi below whom live the Parsioi (Πάρσιοι). The southern regions are inhabited by the Parsiētai (Παρσιῆται), the eastern regions by the Ambautai. The towns and villages lying in the country of the Paropanisadai are these: Parsiana Zarzaua/Barzaura Artoarta Baborana Kapisa niphanda"

— Ptolemy, 150 CE, 6.18.3–4

[Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), the Greek geographer, in the [Geographica](/wiki/Geographica "Geographica") (written between 43 BC to 23 AD) makes mention of the Scythian tribe Pasiani (Πασιανοί), which has also been identified with Pashtuns given that Pashto is an [Eastern-Iranian](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") language, much like the [Scythian languages](/wiki/Scythian_languages "Scythian languages"):[[98]](#cite_note-110)[[99]](#cite_note-Sagar1992-111)[[100]](#cite_note-112)[[101]](#cite_note-Onomasiological-113)[[102]](#cite_note-114)

> "Most of the Scythians...each separate tribe has its peculiar name. All, or the greatest part of them, are nomades. The best known tribes are those who deprived the Greeks of Bactriana, the [Asii](/wiki/Asii "Asii"), Pasiani, [Tochari](/wiki/Tochari "Tochari"), and Sacarauli, who came from the country on the other side of the Iaxartes (Syr Darya)"

— Strabo, The Geography, Book XI, Chapter 8, Section 2

This is considered a different rendering of Ptolemy's Parsioi (Πάρσιοι).[[101]](#cite_note-Onomasiological-113) Johnny Cheung,[[103]](#cite_note-Gnoli_Memorial_Volume-115) reflecting on Ptolemy's *Parsioi (Πάρσιοι)* and Strabo's *Pasiani* (Πασιανοί) states: "Both forms show slight phonetic substitutions, viz. of υ for ι, and the loss of r in Pasianoi is due to perseveration from the preceding Asianoi. They are therefore the most likely candidates as the (linguistic) ancestors of modern day Pashtuns."[[103]](#cite_note-Gnoli_Memorial_Volume-115)

### Middle historical references: Afghan

Further information: [Afghan (ethnonym)](/wiki/Afghan_(ethnonym) "Afghan (ethnonym)")

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") until the advent of modern Afghanistan in the 18th century, the Pashtuns were often referred to as *["Afghans"](/wiki/Afghan_(ethnonym) "Afghan (ethnonym)")*.[[m]](#cite_note-116)
The [etymological](/wiki/Etymological "Etymological") view supported by numerous noted scholars is that the name *Afghan* evidently derives from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") *[Aśvakan](/wiki/A%C5%9Bvaka "Aśvaka")*, or the [Assakenoi](/wiki/Assakenoi "Assakenoi") of [Arrian](/wiki/Arrian "Arrian"), which was the name used for ancient inhabitants of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush").[[104]](#cite_note-117) *Aśvakan* literally means "horsemen", "horse breeders", or "[cavalrymen](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry")" (from *[aśva](/wiki/A%C5%9Bva "Aśva")* or *aspa*, the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") words for "[horse](/wiki/Horse "Horse")").[[105]](#cite_note-118) This view was propounded by scholars like [Christian Lassen](/wiki/Christian_Lassen "Christian Lassen"),[[106]](#cite_note-119) [J. W. McCrindle](/wiki/J._W._McCrindle "J. W. McCrindle"),[[107]](#cite_note-r0-120) M. V. de Saint Martin,[[108]](#cite_note-121) and [É. Reclus](/wiki/%C3%89lis%C3%A9e_Reclus "Élisée Reclus"),[[109]](#cite_note-122)[[110]](#cite_note-r1-123)[[111]](#cite_note-r2-124)[[112]](#cite_note-125)[[113]](#cite_note-r3-126)[[114]](#cite_note-127)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Bactrian_document_Northern_Afghanistan_4th_century.jpg/250px-Bactrian_document_Northern_Afghanistan_4th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bactrian_document_Northern_Afghanistan_4th_century.jpg)

Bactrian document in the Greek script from the 4th century mentioning the word Afghan (αβγανανο): "To Ormuzd Bunukan from Bredag Watanan, the chief of the Afghans"

The earliest mention of the name *Afghan* (*Abgân*) is by [Shapur I](/wiki/Shapur_I "Shapur I") of the [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") during the 3rd century CE,[[115]](#cite_note-Abgan-128) In the 4th century the word "Afghans/Afghana" (αβγανανο) as a reference to a particular people is mentioned in the [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") documents found in Northern Afghanistan.[[116]](#cite_note-Barkhuis-129)[[117]](#cite_note-Bactrian_documents_from_northern_Af-130)

> "To Ormuzd Bunukan, from Bredag Watanan ... greetings and homage from ... ), the ( sotang ( ? ) of Parpaz ( under ) [ the glorious ) [yabghu of Hephthal](/wiki/Tokhara_Yabghus "Tokhara Yabghus"), the chief of the Afghans, ' the judge of [Tukharistan](/wiki/Tokharistan "Tokharistan") and [Gharchistan](/wiki/Gharchistan "Gharchistan") . Moreover, ' a letter [ has come hither ] from you, so I have heard how [ you have ] written ' ' to me concerning ] my health . I arrived in good health, ( and ) ( afterwards ( ? ) ' ' I heard that a message ] was sent thither to you ( saying ) thus : ... look after the farming but the order was given to you thus. You should hand over the grain and then request it from the citizens store: I will not order, so.....I Myself order And I in Respect of winter sends men thither to you then look after the farming, To Ormuzd Bunukan, Greetings"

— the Bactrian documents, 4th century

> "because [you] (pl.), the clan of the Afghans, said thus to me:...And you should not have denied? the men of Rob[[118]](#cite_note-131) [that] the Afghans took (away) the horses"

— the Bactrian documents, 4th century, Sims-Williams 2007b, pp. 90–91

> "[To ...]-bid the Afghan... Moreover, they are in [War]nu(?) because of the Afghans, so [you should] impose a penalty on Nat Kharagan ... ...lord of Warnu with ... ... ...the Afghan... ... "

— the Bactrian documents, 4th century, Sims-Williams 2007b, pp. 90–91

The name Afghan is later recorded in the 6th century CE in the form of *"Avagāṇa"* [अवगाण][[119]](#cite_note-132) by the Indian astronomer [Varāha Mihira](/wiki/Varahamihira "Varahamihira") in his [Brihat-samhita](/wiki/B%E1%B9%9Bhat_Sa%E1%B9%83hit%C4%81 "Bṛhat Saṃhitā").[[120]](#cite_note-133)[[121]](#cite_note-Bhat-134)

> "It would be unfavourable to the people of Chola, the Afghans (Avagāṇa), the white Huns and the Chinese."[[121]](#cite_note-Bhat-134)

— Varāha Mihira, 6th century CE, chapt. 11, verse 61

The word Afghan also appeared in the 982 [Ḥudūd al-ʿĀlam](/wiki/Hudud_ul-%27alam "Hudud ul-'alam"), where a reference is made to a village, Saul, which was probably located near [Gardez](/wiki/Gardez "Gardez"), Afghanistan.[[122]](#cite_note-Vogelsang-135)

> "Saul, a pleasant village on a mountain. In it live *Afghans*".[[122]](#cite_note-Vogelsang-135)

The same book also speaks of a king in Ninhar ([Nangarhar](/wiki/Nangarhar_Province "Nangarhar Province")), who had Muslim, *Afghan* and Hindu wives.[[123]](#cite_note-136) In the 11th century, Afghans are mentioned in [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni")'s *Tarikh-ul Hind* ("History of the Indus"), which describes groups of rebellious Afghans in the tribal lands west of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") in what is today Pakistan.[[122]](#cite_note-Vogelsang-135)[[124]](#cite_note-137)

Al-Utbi, the [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") chronicler, in his *[Tarikh-i Yamini](/wiki/Tarikh_Yamini "Tarikh Yamini")* recorded that many Afghans and Khiljis (possibly the modern [Ghilji](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji")) enlisted in the army of [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin") after [Jayapala](/wiki/Jayapala "Jayapala") was defeated.[[125]](#cite_note-Farishta-2-138) Al-Utbi further stated that Afghans and Ghiljis made a part of [Mahmud Ghaznavi](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni")'s army and were sent on his expedition to [Tocharistan](/wiki/Tocharistan "Tocharistan"), while on another occasion Mahmud Ghaznavi attacked and punished a group of opposing Afghans, as also corroborated by [Abulfazl Beyhaqi](/wiki/Abulfazl_Beyhaqi "Abulfazl Beyhaqi").[[126]](#cite_note-139) It is recorded that Afghans were also enrolled in the [Ghurid Kingdom](/wiki/Ghurid_dynasty "Ghurid dynasty") (1148–1215).[[127]](#cite_note-140) By the beginning of the [Khilji dynasty](/wiki/Khilji_dynasty "Khilji dynasty") in 1290, Afghans have been well known in northern India.

[Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta"), when visiting Afghanistan following the era of the Khilji dynasty, also wrote about the Afghans.

> "We travelled on to Kabul, formerly a vast town, the site of which is now occupied by Afghans. They hold mountains and defiles and possess considerable strength, and are mostly highwaymen. Their principal mountain is called [Kuh Sulayman](/wiki/Sulaiman_Mountains "Sulaiman Mountains"). It is told that the [prophet Sulayman [Solomon]](/wiki/Islamic_view_of_Solomon "Islamic view of Solomon") ascended this mountain and having looked out over India, which was then covered with darkness, returned without entering it."[[128]](#cite_note-141)

— [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta"), 1333

[Ferishta](/wiki/Ferishta "Ferishta"), a 16th-century [Muslim historian](/wiki/Muslim_historians "Muslim historians") writing about the history of [Muslim rule in the subcontinent](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent"), stated:

> He [Khalid bin Abdullah son of [Khalid bin Walid](/wiki/Khalid_bin_Walid "Khalid bin Walid")] retired, therefore, with his family, and a number of Arab retainers, into the Sulaiman Mountains, situated between Multan and Peshawar, where he took up his residence, and gave his daughter in marriage to one of the Afghan chiefs, who had become a proselyte to Mahomedism. From this marriage many children were born, among whom were two sons famous in history. The one [Lodhi](/wiki/Lodi_(Pashtun_tribe) "Lodi (Pashtun tribe)"), the other [Sur](/wiki/Sur_(Pashtun) "Sur (Pashtun)"); who each, subsequently, became head of the tribes which to this day bear their name. I have read in the *Mutla-ul-Anwar*, a work written by a respectable author, and which I procured at [Burhanpur](/wiki/Burhanpur "Burhanpur"), a town of [Khandesh](/wiki/Khandesh "Khandesh") in the [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau"), that the Afghans are [Copts](/wiki/Copt "Copt") of the race of the [Pharaohs](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh"); and that when the prophet Moses got the better of that infidel who was overwhelmed in the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea"), many of the Copts became converts to the [Jewish faith](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"); but others, stubborn and self-willed, refusing to embrace the true faith, leaving their country, came to India, and eventually settled in the Sulimany mountains, where they bore the name of Afghans.[[33]](#cite_note-Ferishta-40)

## History and origins

Main article: [Theories of Pashtun origin](/wiki/Theories_of_Pashtun_origin "Theories of Pashtun origin")

Further information: [History of Afghanistan](/wiki/History_of_Afghanistan "History of Afghanistan"), [History of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/History_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "History of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), [History of Balochistan](/wiki/History_of_Balochistan "History of Balochistan"), and [History of Indian subcontinent](/wiki/History_of_Indian_subcontinent "History of Indian subcontinent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Afghanistan_region_during_500_BC.jpg/250px-Afghanistan_region_during_500_BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghanistan_region_during_500_BC.jpg)

The *[Arachosia](/wiki/Arachosia "Arachosia")* [Satrapy](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap") and the *[Pactyan people](/wiki/Pakthas "Pakthas")* during the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") in 500 BCE

The [ethnogenesis](/wiki/Ethnogenesis "Ethnogenesis") of the Pashtun ethnic group is unclear. There are many conflicting theories among historians and the Pashtuns themselves. Modern scholars believe that Pashtuns do not all share the same origin. The early ancestors of modern-day Pashtuns may have belonged to old [Iranian tribes](/wiki/Iranian_tribes "Iranian tribes") that spread throughout the eastern [Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau").[[129]](#cite_note-142)[[n]](#cite_note-143)[[29]](#cite_note-Brit-Pashtun-36) Historians have also come across references to various ancient [Indo-Aryan tribes](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_tribes "Indo-Aryan tribes") called *[Pakthas](/wiki/Pakthas "Pakthas")* (*Pactyans*) between the [2nd](/wiki/2nd_millennium_BC "2nd millennium BC") and the 1st millennium BC,[[130]](#cite_note-Nath-144)[[131]](#cite_note-145)[[91]](#cite_note-Heredotus-103) although according to [Richard N. Frye](/wiki/Richard_N._Frye "Richard N. Frye") the identification of Pashtuns with the Pakhtas is a mere guess and not proven. And scholars such as [Georg Morgenstierne](/wiki/Georg_Morgenstierne "Georg Morgenstierne") propose the derivation of Pashto from [Parsa](/wiki/Parsii_(tribe) "Parsii (tribe)") or Parswana.[[132]](#cite_note-146)

[Mohan Lal](/wiki/Mohan_Lal_Kashmiri "Mohan Lal Kashmiri") stated in 1846 that "the origin of the [Afghans](/wiki/Afghans "Afghans") is so obscure, that no one, even among the oldest and most clever of the tribe, can give satisfactory information on this point."[[133]](#cite_note-147) Others have suggested that a single origin of the Pashtuns is unlikely but rather they are a tribal confederation.

> "Looking for the origin of Pashtuns and the Afghans is something like exploring the source of the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_Rainforest "Amazon Rainforest"). Is there one specific beginning? And are the Pashtuns originally identical with the Afghans? Although the Pashtuns nowadays constitute a clear ethnic group with their own language and culture, there is no evidence whatsoever that all modern Pashtuns share the same ethnic origin. In fact it is highly unlikely."[[122]](#cite_note-Vogelsang-135)

— [Vogelsang](/wiki/Willem_Vogelsang "Willem Vogelsang"), 2002

### Linguistic origin

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Saka_warrior_Termez_Achaeological_Museum.jpg/250px-Saka_warrior_Termez_Achaeological_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saka_warrior_Termez_Achaeological_Museum.jpg)

[Sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") of a [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") warrior in [Termez](/wiki/Termez "Termez"), [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan")

Pashto is generally classified as an [Eastern Iranian](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian "Eastern Iranian") language.[[o]](#cite_note-149)[[135]](#cite_note-150)[[136]](#cite_note-151) It shares features with the [Munji language](/wiki/Munji_language "Munji language"), which is the closest existing language to the extinct [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactrian_language "Bactrian language"),[[137]](#cite_note-152) but also shares features with the [Sogdian language](/wiki/Sogdian_language "Sogdian language"), as well as [Khwarezmian](/wiki/Khwarezmian_language "Khwarezmian language"), [Shughni](/wiki/Shughni_language "Shughni language"), [Sanglechi](/wiki/Sanglechi_language "Sanglechi language"), and [Khotanese Saka](/wiki/Khotanese_language "Khotanese language").[[p]](#cite_note-153)

It is suggested by some that Pashto may have originated in the [Badakhshan](/wiki/Badakhshan "Badakhshan") region and is connected to a [Saka language](/wiki/Saka_language "Saka language") akin to Khotanese.[[q]](#cite_note-154) In fact major linguist [Georg Morgenstierne](/wiki/Georg_Morgenstierne "Georg Morgenstierne") has described Pashto as a [Saka](/wiki/Saka_language "Saka language") dialect and many others have observed the similarities between Pashto and other Saka languages as well, suggesting that the original Pashto speakers might have been a [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") group.[[138]](#cite_note-155)[[139]](#cite_note-156) Furthermore, Pashto and [Ossetian](/wiki/Ossetian_language "Ossetian language"), another Scythian-descending language, share cognates in their vocabulary which other Eastern Iranian languages lack[[140]](#cite_note-157) Cheung suggests a common isogloss between Pashto and Ossetian which he explains by an undocumented Saka dialect being spoken close to reconstructed Old Pashto which was likely spoken north of the [Oxus](/wiki/Amu_Darya "Amu Darya") at that time.[[141]](#cite_note-158) Others however have suggested a much older Iranic ancestor given the affinity to [Old Avestan](/wiki/Old_Avestan "Old Avestan").[[r]](#cite_note-159)

### Diverse origin

According to one school of thought, Pashtun are descended from a variety of ethnicities, including [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks"), [Turks](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people"), [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs"), [Bactrians](/wiki/Bactrians "Bactrians"), [Dards](/wiki/Dards "Dards"), [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians"), [Tartars](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars"), Huns ([Hephthalites](/wiki/Hephthalites "Hephthalites")), [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols"), [Moghals](/wiki/Moghol_people "Moghol people") (Mughals), and anyone else who has crossed the region where these Pashtun live. Unexpectedly, this includes alleged ties of [Israelite](/wiki/Israelite "Israelite") descent.[[142]](#cite_note-160)[[s]](#cite_note-161)

Some Pashtun tribes claim descent from [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs"), including some claiming to be [Sayyids](/wiki/Sayyids "Sayyids").[[143]](#cite_note-ISBN_Olaf_Caroe-162)

One historical account connects the Pashtuns to a possible [Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") past but this lacks supporting evidence.[[144]](#cite_note-163)

[Henry Walter Bellew](/wiki/Henry_Walter_Bellew "Henry Walter Bellew"), who wrote extensively on Afghan culture, noted that some people claim that the [Bangash](/wiki/Bangash "Bangash") Pashtuns are connected to [Ismail Samani](/wiki/Ismail_Samani "Ismail Samani").[[145]](#cite_note-164)

### Greek origin

According to Firasat et al. 2007, a proportion of Pashtuns may descend from Greeks, but they also suggest that Greek ancestry may also have come from Greek slaves brought by [Xerxes I](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I").[[146]](#cite_note-165)

The Greek ancestry of the Pashtuns may also be traced on the basis of a homologous group. And [Haplogroup J2](/wiki/Haplogroup_J-M172 "Haplogroup J-M172") is from the Semitic population, and this haplogroup is found in 6.5% of Greeks and Pashtuns and 55.6% of the Israelite population.[[147]](#cite_note-166)

A number of genetic studies on *Pashtuns* have lately been undertaken by academics from various institutions and research institutes. The Greek heritage of Pakistani Pashtuns has been researched in. In this study, the *Pashtuns*, *Kalash*, and *Burusho* to be descended from Alexander's soldiers considered.[[148]](#cite_note-167)

[Henry Walter Bellew](/wiki/Henry_Walter_Bellew "Henry Walter Bellew") (1834–1892) was of the view that the Pashtuns likely have mixed [Greek and Indian Rajput roots](/wiki/Theory_of_Pashtun_descent_from_Rajputs "Theory of Pashtun descent from Rajputs").[[149]](#cite_note-Quddus1987-168)[[150]](#cite_note-169)

Following Alexander's brief occupation, the successor state of the [Seleucid Empire](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire") expanded influence on the Pashtuns until 305 BCE when they gave up dominating power to the Indian [Maurya Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") as part of an alliance treaty.[[151]](#cite_note-170)

Some groups from [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") and [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar") believe to be descended from [Greeks](/wiki/Greek_people "Greek people") who arrived with [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great").[[152]](#cite_note-Greek_ancestry-171)

### Hephthalite origin

According to some accounts the [Ghilji](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji") tribe has been connected to the [Khalaj people](/wiki/Khalaj_people "Khalaj people").[[153]](#cite_note-Minorsky-172) Following [al-Khwarizmi](/wiki/Al-Khwarizmi "Al-Khwarizmi"), [Josef Markwart](/wiki/Josef_Markwart "Josef Markwart") claimed the Khalaj to be remnants of the [Hephthalite](/wiki/Hephthalite "Hephthalite") confederacy.[[154]](#cite_note-iri1-173) The Hephthalites may have been Indo-Iranian,[[154]](#cite_note-iri1-173) although the view that they were of [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") [Gaoju](/wiki/Tiele_people "Tiele people") origin[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_la_Vaissière2003119–137-174) "seems to be most prominent at present".[[156]](#cite_note-175) The Khalaj may originally have been Turkic-speaking and only federated with Iranian Pashto-speaking tribes in medieval times.[[157]](#cite_note-176)

However, according to linguist [Sims-Williams](/wiki/Nicholas_Sims-Williams "Nicholas Sims-Williams"), archaeological documents do not support the suggestion that the Khalaj were the successors of the Hephthalites,[[158]](#cite_note-177) while according to historian [V. Minorsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Minorsky "Vladimir Minorsky"), the Khalaj were "perhaps only politically associated with the Hephthalites."[[153]](#cite_note-Minorsky-172)

According to [Georg Morgenstierne](/wiki/Georg_Morgenstierne "Georg Morgenstierne"), the [Durrani](/wiki/Durrani "Durrani") tribe who were known as the "Abdali" before the formation of the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") 1747,[[159]](#cite_note-178) might be connected to with the [Hephthalites](/wiki/Hephthalites "Hephthalites");[[160]](#cite_note-179) [Aydogdy Kurbanov](/wiki/Aydogdy_Kurbanov "Aydogdy Kurbanov") endorses this view who proposes that after the collapse of the Hephthalite confederacy, Hephthalite likely assimilated into different local populations.[[161]](#cite_note-180)

According to [The Cambridge History of Iran](/wiki/The_Cambridge_History_of_Iran "The Cambridge History of Iran") volume 3, Issue 1, the [Ghilji](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji") tribe of Afghanistan are the descendants of Hephthalites.[[162]](#cite_note-181)

### Anthropology and oral traditions

#### Theory of Pashtun descent from Israelites

Main article: [Theory of Pashtun descent from Israelites](/wiki/Theory_of_Pashtun_descent_from_Israelites "Theory of Pashtun descent from Israelites")

Some [anthropologists](/wiki/Anthropologist "Anthropologist") lend credence to the [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") of the Pashtun tribes themselves. For example, according to the *[Encyclopaedia of Islam](/wiki/Encyclopaedia_of_Islam "Encyclopaedia of Islam")*, the theory of Pashtun descent from Israelites is traced to [Nimat Allah al-Harawi](/wiki/Nimat_Allah_al-Harawi "Nimat Allah al-Harawi"), who compiled a history for *[Khan-e-Jehan Lodhi](/wiki/Khan_Jahan_Lodi "Khan Jahan Lodi")* in the reign of [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") Emperor [Jehangir](/wiki/Jehangir "Jehangir") in the 17th century.[[163]](#cite_note-Houtsma-150-182) The 13th century [Tabaqat-i Nasiri](/wiki/Tabaqat-i_Nasiri "Tabaqat-i Nasiri") discusses the settlement of immigrant Bani Israel at the end of the 8th century CE in the [Ghor region](/wiki/Ghor_Province "Ghor Province") of Afghanistan, settlement attested by Jewish inscriptions in Ghor. Historian André Wink suggests that the story "may contain a clue to the remarkable theory of the Jewish origin of some of the Afghan tribes which is persistently advocated in the Persian-Afghan chronicles."[[164]](#cite_note-Wink-183) These references to Bani Israel agree with the commonly held view by Pashtuns that when the twelve tribes of Israel were dispersed, the [tribe of Joseph](/wiki/Tribe_of_Joseph "Tribe of Joseph"), among other Hebrew tribes, settled in the Afghanistan region.[[165]](#cite_note-Jewish-library-184) This oral tradition is widespread among the Pashtun tribes. There have been many legends over the centuries of descent from the [Ten Lost Tribes](/wiki/Ten_Lost_Tribes "Ten Lost Tribes") after groups converted to Christianity and Islam. Hence the tribal name [Yusufzai](/wiki/Yusufzai "Yusufzai") in Pashto translates to the "son of Joseph". A similar story is told by many historians, including the 14th century [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta") and 16th century [Ferishta](/wiki/Firishta "Firishta").[[33]](#cite_note-Ferishta-40) However, the similarity of names can also be traced to the presence of Arabic through Islam.[[166]](#cite_note-Stanizai2020-185)

This theory of Pashtuns Jewish origin has been largely denied and is said that Its biblical claims are anecdotal, its historical documentation is inconsistent, its geographic claims are incoherent, and its linguistic assertions are implausible.[[167]](#cite_note-186)

One conflicting issue in the belief that the Pashtuns descend from the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") is that the Ten Lost Tribes were exiled by the ruler of [Assyria](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria"), while *[Maghzan-e-Afghani](/wiki/Maghzan-e-Afghani "Maghzan-e-Afghani")* says they were permitted by the ruler to go east to Afghanistan. This inconsistency can be explained by the fact that Persia acquired the lands of the ancient Assyrian Empire when it conquered the Empire of the Medes and Chaldean [Babylonia](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia"), which had conquered Assyria decades earlier. But no ancient author mentions such a transfer of Israelites further east, or no ancient extra-Biblical texts refer to the Ten Lost Tribes at all.[[168]](#cite_note-187)

Some Afghan historians have maintained that Pashtuns are [linked to the ancient Israelites](/wiki/Theory_of_Pashtun_descent_from_the_Israelites "Theory of Pashtun descent from the Israelites"). [Mohan Lal](/wiki/Mohan_Lal_(Zutshi) "Mohan Lal (Zutshi)") quoted [Mountstuart Elphinstone](/wiki/Mountstuart_Elphinstone "Mountstuart Elphinstone") who wrote:

> "The Afghan historians proceed to relate that the children of Israel, both in [Ghore](/wiki/Ghor "Ghor") and in Arabia, preserved their knowledge of the unity of [God](/wiki/Allah "Allah") and the purity of their religious belief, and that on the appearance of the last and greatest of the prophets ([Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")) the Afghans of Ghore listened to the invitation of their Arabian brethren, the chief of whom was [Khauled](/wiki/Khalid_ibn_al-Walid "Khalid ibn al-Walid")...if we consider the easy way with which all rude nations receive accounts favourable to their own antiquity, I fear we much class the descents of the Afghans from the Jews with that of the Romans and the British from the Trojans, and that of the Irish from the Milesians or Brahmins."[[169]](#cite_note-Lal-188)

— Mountstuart Elphinstone, 1841

This theory has been criticized for not being substantiated by historical evidence.[[166]](#cite_note-Stanizai2020-185) Zaman Stanizai criticizes this theory:[[166]](#cite_note-Stanizai2020-185)

> "The 'mythified' misconception that the Pashtuns are the descendants of the lost tribes of Israel is a fabrication popularized in 14th-century India. A claim that is full of logical inconsistencies and historical incongruities, and stands in stark contrast to the conclusive evidence of the Indo-Iranian origin of Pashtuns supported by the incontrovertible DNA sequencing that the genome analysis revealed scientifically."

— [[166]](#cite_note-Stanizai2020-185)

According to genetic studies Pashtuns have a greater [R1a1a\*-M198](/wiki/Haplogroup_R1a "Haplogroup R1a") modal halogroup than Jews:[[170]](#cite_note-pmid22510847-189)

> "Our study demonstrates genetic similarities between Pathans from Afghanistan and Pakistan, both of which are characterized by the predominance of haplogroup R1a1a\*-M198 (>50%) and the sharing of the same modal haplotype...Although Greeks and Jews have been proposed as ancestors to Pathans, their genetic origin remains ambiguous...Overall, Ashkenazi Jews exhibit a frequency of 15.3% for haplogroup R1a1a-M198"

— "Afghanistan from a Y-chromosome perspective", *European Journal of Human Genetics*

## Modern era

See also: [Pashtun nationalism](/wiki/Pashtun_nationalism "Pashtun nationalism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Sher_Ali_Khan_and_company_of_Afghanistan_in_1869.jpg/250px-Sher_Ali_Khan_and_company_of_Afghanistan_in_1869.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sher_Ali_Khan_and_company_of_Afghanistan_in_1869.jpg)

[Afghan Amir](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Afghanistan "List of monarchs of Afghanistan") [Sher Ali Khan](/wiki/Sher_Ali_Khan "Sher Ali Khan") (in the center with his son) and his delegation in [Ambala](/wiki/Ambala "Ambala"), near [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), in 1869

Their modern past stretches back to the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") ([Khalji](/wiki/Khalji_dynasty "Khalji dynasty") and [Lodi dynasty](/wiki/Lodi_dynasty "Lodi dynasty")), the [Hotak dynasty](/wiki/Hotak_dynasty "Hotak dynasty") and the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire"). The Hotak rulers rebelled against the [Safavids](/wiki/Safavids "Safavids") and seized control over much of Persia from 1722 to 1729.[[171]](#cite_note-Browne-190) This was followed by the conquests of [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani") who was a former high-ranking military commander under [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah") and founder of the Durrani Empire, which covered most of what is now Afghanistan, Pakistan, [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir_region "Kashmir region"), [Indian Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), as well as the [Kohistan](/wiki/Quhistan "Quhistan") and [Khorasan](/wiki/Khorasan_province "Khorasan province") provinces of Iran.[[172]](#cite_note-Last-Afghan-empire-191) After the decline of the [Durrani dynasty](/wiki/Durrani_dynasty "Durrani dynasty") in the first half of the 19th century under [Shuja Shah Durrani](/wiki/Shuja_Shah_Durrani "Shuja Shah Durrani"), the [Barakzai dynasty](/wiki/Barakzai_dynasty "Barakzai dynasty") took control of the empire. Specifically, the [Mohamedzais](/wiki/Mohamedzai "Mohamedzai") held Afghanistan's monarchy from around 1826 to the end of [Zahir Shah](/wiki/Mohammed_Zahir_Shah "Mohammed Zahir Shah")'s reign in 1973.

During the so-called "[Great Game](/wiki/The_Great_Game "The Great Game")" of the 19th century, rivalry between the [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") empires was useful to the Pashtuns of Afghanistan in resisting foreign control and retaining a degree of autonomy (see the [Siege of Malakand](/wiki/Siege_of_Malakand "Siege of Malakand")). However, during the reign of [Abdur Rahman Khan](/wiki/Abdur_Rahman_Khan "Abdur Rahman Khan") (1880–1901), [Pashtun regions](/wiki/Pashtunistan "Pashtunistan") were [politically](/wiki/Politically "Politically") divided by the [Durand Line](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line") – areas that would become western Pakistan fell within British India as a result of the border.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan_and_Gandhi_in_1940.jpg/250px-Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan_and_Gandhi_in_1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan_and_Gandhi_in_1940.jpg)

Leader of the [non-violent](/wiki/Nonviolence "Nonviolence") [Khudai Khidmatgar](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgar "Khudai Khidmatgar"), also referred to as "the Red shirts" movement, [Bacha Khan](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan "Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan"), standing with [Mohandas Gandhi](/wiki/Mohandas_Karamchand_Gandhi "Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi")

In the 20th century, many politically active Pashtun leaders living under British rule of undivided India supported [Indian independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"), including [Ashfaqulla Khan](/wiki/Ashfaqulla_Khan "Ashfaqulla Khan"),[[173]](#cite_note-192)[[174]](#cite_note-Rajesh-193) [Abdul Samad Khan Achakzai](/wiki/Abdul_Samad_Khan_Achakzai "Abdul Samad Khan Achakzai"), [Ajmal Khattak](/wiki/Ajmal_Khattak "Ajmal Khattak"), [Bacha Khan](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan "Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan") and his son [Wali Khan](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Wali_Khan "Khan Abdul Wali Khan") (both members of the [Khudai Khidmatgar](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgar "Khudai Khidmatgar")), and were inspired by [Mohandas Gandhi](/wiki/Mohandas_Gandhi "Mohandas Gandhi")'s [non-violent](/wiki/Nonviolence "Nonviolence") method of resistance.[[175]](#cite_note-Brit-Ghaffar-Khan-194)[[176]](#cite_note-195) Many Pashtuns also worked in the [Muslim League](/wiki/All_India_Muslim_League "All India Muslim League") to fight for an independent Pakistan through non violent resistance, including [Yusuf Khattak](/wiki/Yusuf_Khattak "Yusuf Khattak") and [Abdur Rab Nishtar](/wiki/Sardar_Abdur_Rab_Nishtar "Sardar Abdur Rab Nishtar") who was a close associate of [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah").[[177]](#cite_note-iloveindia-196)
The Pashtuns of Afghanistan attained complete independence from British [political intervention](/wiki/Interventionism_(politics) "Interventionism (politics)") during the reign of [Amanullah Khan](/wiki/Amanullah_Khan "Amanullah Khan"), following the [Third Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/European_influence_in_Afghanistan#Third_Anglo-Afghan_War_and_Independence "European influence in Afghanistan"). By the 1950s a popular call for [Pashtunistan](/wiki/Pashtunistan "Pashtunistan") began to be heard in Afghanistan and the new state of Pakistan. This led to bad relations between the two nations. The Afghan monarchy ended when [President](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_Afghanistan "List of Presidents of Afghanistan") [Daoud Khan](/wiki/Mohammed_Daoud_Khan "Mohammed Daoud Khan") [seized control of Afghanistan](/wiki/1973_Afghan_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1973 Afghan coup d'état") from his cousin [Zahir Shah](/wiki/Zahir_Shah "Zahir Shah") in 1973 on a [Pashtun Nationalist](/wiki/Pashtun_nationalism "Pashtun nationalism") agenda, which opened doors for a [proxy war](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") by neighbours. In April 1978, [Daoud Khan was assassinated](/wiki/Saur_Revolution "Saur Revolution") along with his family and relatives in a bloody coup orchestrated by [Hafizullah Amin](/wiki/Hafizullah_Amin "Hafizullah Amin"). [Afghan mujahideen](/wiki/Afghan_mujahideen "Afghan mujahideen") commanders in exile in neighbouring Pakistan began recruiting for a [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") against the [Democratic Republic of Afghanistan](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Democratic Republic of Afghanistan") – the Marxist government which was also dominated by Pashtun [Khalqists](/wiki/Khalq "Khalq") who held [Nationalist](/wiki/Pashtun_nationalism "Pashtun nationalism") views including [Hafizullah Amin](/wiki/Hafizullah_Amin "Hafizullah Amin"), [Nur Muhammad Taraki](/wiki/Nur_Muhammad_Taraki "Nur Muhammad Taraki"), General [Mohammad Aslam Vatanjar](/wiki/Mohammad_Aslam_Watanjar "Mohammad Aslam Watanjar"), [Shahnawaz Tanai](/wiki/Shahnawaz_Tanai "Shahnawaz Tanai"), [Mohammad Gulabzoy](/wiki/Sayed_Mohammad_Gulabzoy "Sayed Mohammad Gulabzoy") and many more. In 1979, the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") intervened in its southern neighbour Afghanistan in order to defeat a rising insurgency. The Afghan mujahideen were funded by the United States, Saudi Arabia, China and others, and included some Pashtun commanders such as [Abdul Rasul Sayyaf](/wiki/Abdul_Rasul_Sayyaf "Abdul Rasul Sayyaf"), [Gulbuddin Hekmatyar](/wiki/Gulbuddin_Hekmatyar "Gulbuddin Hekmatyar"), [Jalaluddin Haqqani](/wiki/Jalaluddin_Haqqani "Jalaluddin Haqqani"), [Mohammad Nabi Mohammadi](/wiki/Mohammad_Nabi_Mohammadi "Mohammad Nabi Mohammadi") and [Mohammad Yunus Khalis](/wiki/Mohammad_Yunus_Khalis "Mohammad Yunus Khalis"). In the meantime, millions of Pashtuns joined the [Afghan diaspora](/wiki/Afghan_diaspora "Afghan diaspora") in [Pakistan](/wiki/Afghans_in_Pakistan "Afghans in Pakistan") and [Iran](/wiki/Afghans_in_Iran "Afghans in Iran"), and from there tens of thousands proceeded to Europe, North America, Oceania and other parts of the world.[[178]](#cite_note-wider.unu.edu-197) The Afghan government and [military](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of Afghanistan") would remain predominantly Pashtun until the fall of [Mohammad Najibullah's](/wiki/Mohammad_Najibullah "Mohammad Najibullah") [Republic of Afghanistan](/wiki/Republic_of_Afghanistan_(1978-1992) "Republic of Afghanistan (1978-1992)") in April 1992.[[179]](#cite_note-198)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Secretary_Pompeo_Meets_with_the_Taliban_Negotiation_Team_%2850632321483%29.jpg/250px-Secretary_Pompeo_Meets_with_the_Taliban_Negotiation_Team_%2850632321483%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Pompeo_Meets_with_the_Taliban_Negotiation_Team_(50632321483).jpg)

American diplomat [Zalmay Khalilzad](/wiki/Zalmay_Khalilzad "Zalmay Khalilzad") with the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") officials [Abdul Ghani Baradar](/wiki/Abdul_Ghani_Baradar "Abdul Ghani Baradar"), [Abdul Hakim Ishaqzai](/wiki/Abdul_Hakim_Ishaqzai "Abdul Hakim Ishaqzai"), [Sher Mohammad Abbas Stanikzai](/wiki/Sher_Mohammad_Abbas_Stanikzai "Sher Mohammad Abbas Stanikzai") and [Suhail Shaheen](/wiki/Suhail_Shaheen "Suhail Shaheen")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Imran_Khan_WEF.jpg/250px-Imran_Khan_WEF.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imran_Khan_WEF.jpg)

[Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan "Imran Khan"), Pakistani cricketer-turned-politician and former Prime Minister, belongs to the [Niazi](/wiki/Niazi "Niazi") tribe.

Many high-ranking government officials in the [Islamic Republic of Afghanistan](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Islamic Republic of Afghanistan") were Pashtuns, including: [Abdul Rahim Wardak](/wiki/Abdul_Rahim_Wardak "Abdul Rahim Wardak"), [Abdul Salam Azimi](/wiki/Abdul_Salam_Azimi "Abdul Salam Azimi"), [Anwar ul-Haq Ahady](/wiki/Anwar_ul-Haq_Ahady "Anwar ul-Haq Ahady"), [Amirzai Sangin](/wiki/Amirzai_Sangin "Amirzai Sangin"), [Ghulam Farooq Wardak](/wiki/Ghulam_Farooq_Wardak "Ghulam Farooq Wardak"), [Hamid Karzai](/wiki/Hamid_Karzai "Hamid Karzai"), [Mohammad Ishaq Aloko](/wiki/Mohammad_Ishaq_Aloko "Mohammad Ishaq Aloko"), [Omar Zakhilwal](/wiki/Omar_Zakhilwal "Omar Zakhilwal"), [Sher Mohammad Karimi](/wiki/Sher_Mohammad_Karimi "Sher Mohammad Karimi"), [Zalmay Rasoul](/wiki/Zalmay_Rasoul "Zalmay Rasoul"), [Yousef Pashtun](/wiki/Yousef_Pashtun "Yousef Pashtun"). The [list of current governors of Afghanistan](/wiki/List_of_current_governors_of_Afghanistan "List of current governors of Afghanistan") also include large percentage of Pashtuns. [Mullah Yaqoob](/wiki/Mullah_Yaqoob "Mullah Yaqoob") serves as acting Defense Minister, [Sirajuddin Haqqani](/wiki/Sirajuddin_Haqqani "Sirajuddin Haqqani") as acting Interior Minister, [Amir Khan Muttaqi](/wiki/Amir_Khan_Muttaqi "Amir Khan Muttaqi") as acting Foreign Minister, [Gul Agha Ishakzai](/wiki/Gul_Agha_Ishakzai "Gul Agha Ishakzai") as acting Finance Minister, and [Hasan Akhund](/wiki/Hasan_Akhund "Hasan Akhund") as acting Prime Minister. A number of other ministers are also Pashtuns.

The Afghan [royal family](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family"), which was represented by [King Zahir Shah](/wiki/Mohammad_Zahir_Shah "Mohammad Zahir Shah"), are referred to [Mohammadzais](/wiki/Mohammadzai "Mohammadzai"). Other prominent Pashtuns include the 17th-century poets [Khushal Khan Khattak](/wiki/Khushal_Khan_Khattak "Khushal Khan Khattak") and [Rahman Baba](/wiki/Rahman_Baba "Rahman Baba"), and in contemporary era [Afghan Astronaut](/wiki/Astronaut#Terminology "Astronaut") [Abdul Ahad Mohmand](/wiki/Abdul_Ahad_Mohmand "Abdul Ahad Mohmand"), former [US Ambassador](/wiki/United_States_Ambassador_to_the_United_Nations "United States Ambassador to the United Nations") [Zalmay Khalilzad](/wiki/Zalmay_Khalilzad "Zalmay Khalilzad"), and [Ashraf Ghani](/wiki/Ashraf_Ghani "Ashraf Ghani") among many others.

Many Pashtuns of Pakistan and India have adopted non-Pashtun cultures, mainly by abandoning Pashto and using languages such as [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), and [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko_language "Hindko language").[[180]](#cite_note-Hakala2012-199) These include [Ghulam Mohammad](/wiki/Malik_Ghulam_Muhammad "Malik Ghulam Muhammad") (first [Finance Minister](/wiki/Finance_Minister_of_Pakistan "Finance Minister of Pakistan"), from 1947 to 1951, and third [Governor-General of Pakistan](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Pakistan "Governor-General of Pakistan"), from 1951 to 1955),[[181]](#cite_note-express-200)[[182]](#cite_note-pakistanherald-201)[[183]](#cite_note-samaatv-202)[[184]](#cite_note-Kakazai-203)[[185]](#cite_note-Routledge,_Kumarasingham-204) [Ayub Khan](/wiki/Ayub_Khan_(President_of_Pakistan) "Ayub Khan (President of Pakistan)"), who was the second [President of Pakistan](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan"), [Zakir Husain](/wiki/Zakir_Husain_(politician) "Zakir Husain (politician)") who was the third [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") and [Abdul Qadeer Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Qadeer_Khan "Abdul Qadeer Khan"), father of Pakistan's [nuclear weapons program](/wiki/Pakistan_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "Pakistan and weapons of mass destruction").

Many more held high government posts, such as [Asfandyar Wali Khan](/wiki/Asfandyar_Wali_Khan "Asfandyar Wali Khan"), [Mahmood Khan Achakzai](/wiki/Mahmood_Khan_Achakzai "Mahmood Khan Achakzai"), [Sirajul Haq](/wiki/Sirajul_Haq "Sirajul Haq"), and [Aftab Ahmad Sherpao](/wiki/Aftab_Ahmad_Sherpao "Aftab Ahmad Sherpao"), who are presidents of their respective political parties in Pakistan. Others became famous in sports (e.g., [Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan "Imran Khan"), [Mansoor Ali Khan Pataudi](/wiki/Mansoor_Ali_Khan_Pataudi "Mansoor Ali Khan Pataudi"), [Younis Khan](/wiki/Younis_Khan "Younis Khan"), [Shahid Afridi](/wiki/Shahid_Afridi "Shahid Afridi"), [Irfan Pathan](/wiki/Irfan_Pathan "Irfan Pathan"), [Jahangir Khan](/wiki/Jahangir_Khan "Jahangir Khan"), [Jansher Khan](/wiki/Jansher_Khan "Jansher Khan"), [Hashim Khan](/wiki/Hashim_Khan "Hashim Khan"), [Rashid Khan](/wiki/Rashid_Khan_(cricketer) "Rashid Khan (cricketer)"), [Shaheen Afridi](/wiki/Shaheen_Afridi "Shaheen Afridi"), [Naseem Shah](/wiki/Naseem_Shah_(cricketer) "Naseem Shah (cricketer)"), [Misbah Ul Haq](/wiki/Misbah-ul-Haq "Misbah-ul-Haq"), [Mujeeb Ur Rahman](/wiki/Mujeeb_Ur_Rahman "Mujeeb Ur Rahman") and [Mohammad Wasim](/wiki/Mohammad_Wasim_(cricketer,_born_2001) "Mohammad Wasim (cricketer, born 2001)")) and literature (e.g., [Ghani Khan](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Ghani_Khan "Khan Abdul Ghani Khan"), [Hamza Shinwari](/wiki/Hamza_Shinwari "Hamza Shinwari"), and [Kabir Stori](/wiki/Kabir_Stori "Kabir Stori")). [Malala Yousafzai](/wiki/Malala_Yousafzai "Malala Yousafzai"), who became the youngest [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") recipient in [2014](/wiki/2014_Nobel_Peace_Prize "2014 Nobel Peace Prize"), is a Pakistani Pashtun. Pashtuns are considered to be well-integrated in the Pakistani society, and as per a 2009 [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") report 92% of the Pashtuns identified with their Pakistani identity before their ethnic Pashtun identity.[[186]](#cite_note-205)

Many of the [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") film stars in India have Pashtun ancestry; some of the most notable ones are [Aamir Khan](/wiki/Aamir_Khan "Aamir Khan"), [Shahrukh Khan](/wiki/Shah_Rukh_Khan "Shah Rukh Khan"), [Salman Khan](/wiki/Salman_Khan "Salman Khan"), [Feroz Khan](/wiki/Feroz_Khan_(Indian_actor) "Feroz Khan (Indian actor)"), [Madhubala](/wiki/Madhubala "Madhubala"), [Kader Khan](/wiki/Kader_Khan "Kader Khan"), [Saif Ali Khan](/wiki/Saif_Ali_Khan "Saif Ali Khan"), [Soha Ali Khan](/wiki/Soha_Ali_Khan "Soha Ali Khan"), [Sara Ali Khan](/wiki/Sara_Ali_Khan "Sara Ali Khan"), and [Zarine Khan](/wiki/Zarine_Khan "Zarine Khan").[[187]](#cite_note-Devasher2022-206)[[188]](#cite_note-pathan-207) In addition, one of India's former presidents, [Zakir Husain](/wiki/Zakir_Husain "Zakir Husain"), belonged to the [Afridi](/wiki/Afridi_(Pashtun) "Afridi (Pashtun)") tribe.[[189]](#cite_note-208)[[190]](#cite_note-209)[[191]](#cite_note-210) [Mohammad Yunus](/wiki/Mohammad_Yunus_(diplomat) "Mohammad Yunus (diplomat)"), India's former ambassador to Algeria and advisor to [Indira Gandhi](/wiki/Indira_Gandhi "Indira Gandhi"), is of Pashtun origin and related to the legendary Bacha Khan.[[192]](#cite_note-211)[[193]](#cite_note-212)[[194]](#cite_note-213)[[195]](#cite_note-214)

In the late 1990s, Pashtuns were the primary ethnic group in the ruling regime i.e. [Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan](/wiki/Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan_(1996%E2%80%932001) "Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan (1996–2001)") (Taliban regime).[[196]](#cite_note-215)[[197]](#cite_note-BBC_Taliban-216)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The [Northern Alliance](/wiki/Northern_Alliance "Northern Alliance") that was fighting against the Taliban also included a number of Pashtuns. Among them were [Abdullah Abdullah](/wiki/Abdullah_Abdullah "Abdullah Abdullah"), [Abdul Qadir](/wiki/Abdul_Qadir_(Afghan_leader) "Abdul Qadir (Afghan leader)") and his brother [Abdul Haq](/wiki/Abdul_Haq_(Afghan_leader) "Abdul Haq (Afghan leader)"), [Abdul Rasul Sayyaf](/wiki/Abdul_Rasul_Sayyaf "Abdul Rasul Sayyaf"), [Asadullah Khalid](/wiki/Asadullah_Khalid "Asadullah Khalid"), [Hamid Karzai](/wiki/Hamid_Karzai "Hamid Karzai") and [Gul Agha Sherzai](/wiki/Gul_Agha_Sherzai "Gul Agha Sherzai"). The Taliban regime was ousted in late 2001 during the US-led [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom") and replaced by the [Karzai administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Hamid_Karzai "Presidency of Hamid Karzai").[[198]](#cite_note-217) This was followed by the Ghani administration and the reconquest of Afghanistan by the Taliban (Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan).

The long wars in Afghanistan have led to Pashtuns gaining a reputation for being exceptional fighters.[[199]](#cite_note-218) Some activists and intellectuals are trying to rebuild Pashtun intellectualism and its pre-war culture.[[200]](#cite_note-219)

## Genetics

The majority of Pashtuns from Afghanistan belong to [R1a](/wiki/R1a "R1a"), with a frequency of 50–65%.[[201]](#cite_note-Afghanistan_from_a_Y-chromosome_per-220) Subclade R1a-Z2125 occurs at a frequency of 40%.[[202]](#cite_note-nagy-221) This subclade is predominantly found in Tajiks, Turkmen, Uzbeks and in some populations in the Caucasus and Iran.[[203]](#cite_note-222) [Haplogroup G-M201](/wiki/Haplogroup_G-M201 "Haplogroup G-M201") reaches 9% in Afghan Pashtuns and is the second most frequent haplogroup in Pashtuns from southern Afghanistan.[[201]](#cite_note-Afghanistan_from_a_Y-chromosome_per-220)[[204]](#cite_note-223) Haplogroup L and Haplogroup J2 occurs at an overall frequency of 6%.[[201]](#cite_note-Afghanistan_from_a_Y-chromosome_per-220) According to a Mitochondrial DNA analysis of four ethnic groups of Afghanistan, the majority of mtDNA among Afghan Pashtuns belongs to West Eurasian lineages, and share a greater affinity with West Eurasian and Central Asian populations rather than to populations of South Asia or East Asia. The haplogroup analysis indicates the Pashtuns and Tajiks in Afghanistan share ancestral heritage. Among the studied ethnic groups, the Pashtuns have the greatest mtDNA diversity.[[205]](#cite_note-224) The most frequent haplogroup among Pakistani Pashtuns is haplogroup R which is found at a rate of 28–50%. Haplogroup J2 was found in 9% to 24% depending on the study and Haplogroup E has been found at a frequency of 4% to 13%. Haplogroup L occurs at a rate of 8%. Certain Pakistani Pashtun groups exhibit high levels of R1b.[[206]](#cite_note-225)[[207]](#cite_note-226) Overall Pashtun groups are genetically diverse, and the Pashtun ethnic group is not a single genetic population. Different Pashtun groups exhibit different genetic backgrounds, resulting in considerable heterogeneity.[[208]](#cite_note-227)

Y haplogroup and mtdna haplogroup samples were taken from Jadoon, Yousafzai, Sayyid, Gujar and Tanoli men living in [Swabi District](/wiki/Swabi_District "Swabi District"), Khyber Pakhtunkhwa in Pakistan. Jadoon men have predominantly East Asian origin paternal ancestry with West Eurasian maternal ancestry and a lesser amount of South Asian maternal ancestry according to a Y and mtdna haplogroup test indicating local females marrying immigrant males during the medieval period. Y Haplogroup O3-M122 makes up the majority of Jadoon men, the same haplogroup carried by the majority (50–60%) of Han Chinese. 82.5% of Jadoon men carrying Q-MEH2 and O3-M122 which are both of East Asian origin. O3-M122 was absent in the Sayyid (Syed) population and appeared in low numbers among Tanolis, Gujars and [Yousafzais](/wiki/Yousafzai "Yousafzai"). There appears to be founder affect in the O3-M122 among the Jadoon.[[209]](#cite_note-228)[[210]](#cite_note-229)[[211]](#cite_note-230) 76.32% of Jadoon men carry O3-M122 while 0.75% of Tanolis, 0.81% of Gujars and 2.82% of Yousafzais carry O3-M122.[[212]](#cite_note-231)[[213]](#cite_note-232)

56.25% of the Jadoons in another test carried West Eurasian maternal [Haplogroup H (mtDNA)](/wiki/Haplogroup_H_(mtDNA) "Haplogroup H (mtDNA)").[[214]](#cite_note-233) Dental morphology of the Swabi Jadoons was also analysed and compared to other groups in the regions like Yousufzais and Sayyids.[[215]](#cite_note-234)

## Definitions

The most prominent views among Pashtuns as to who exactly qualifies as a Pashtun are:[[216]](#cite_note-Conflict-235)

1. Those who are well-versed in [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") and use it significantly. The Pashto language is "one of the primary markers of ethnic identity" among Pashtuns.[[217]](#cite_note-Brill-236)
2. Adherence to the code of [Pashtunwali](/wiki/Pashtunwali "Pashtunwali").[[216]](#cite_note-Conflict-235)[[218]](#cite_note-237) The cultural definition requires Pashtuns to adhere to Pashtunwali codes.[[219]](#cite_note-238)
3. Belonging to a [Pashtun tribe](/wiki/Pashtun_tribes "Pashtun tribes") through [patrilineal descent](/wiki/Patrilineality "Patrilineality"), based on an important orthodox law of Pashtunwali which mainly requires that only those who have a Pashtun father are Pashtun. This definition places less emphasis on the language.[[220]](#cite_note-239)

### Tribes

Main article: [Pashtun tribes](/wiki/Pashtun_tribes "Pashtun tribes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/CIA_Afghanistan_ethnic_groups_map.jpg/250px-CIA_Afghanistan_ethnic_groups_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:CIA_Afghanistan_ethnic_groups_map.jpg)

A map of Pashtun tribes[[221]](#cite_note-240)

A prominent institution of the Pashtun people is the intricate system of [tribes](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe").[[222]](#cite_note-241) The tribal system has several levels of organization: the tribe they are in is from [four 'greater' tribal groups](/wiki/Qais_Abdur_Rashid#Descendants "Qais Abdur Rashid"): the [Sarbani](/wiki/Sarbani "Sarbani"), the [Bettani](/wiki/Bettani "Bettani"), the Gharghashti, and the [Karlani](/wiki/Karlani "Karlani").[[223]](#cite_note-242) The tribe is then divided into kinship groups called *khels*, which in turn is divided into smaller groups (*pllarina* or *plarganey*), each consisting of several extended families called *kahols*.[[224]](#cite_note-Jirga-243)

#### Durrani and Ghilji Pashtuns

Main articles: [Durrani](/wiki/Durrani "Durrani") and [Ghilji](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji")

The Durranis and Ghiljis (or Ghilzais) are the two largest groups of Pashtuns, with approximately two-thirds of Afghan Pashtuns belonging to these confederations.[[225]](#cite_note-USMA-244) The Durrani tribe has been more urban and politically successful, while the Ghiljis are more numerous, more rural, and reputedly tougher. In the 18th century, the groups collaborated at times and at other times fought each other. With a few gaps, Durranis ruled modern Afghanistan continuously until the [Saur Revolution](/wiki/Saur_Revolution "Saur Revolution") of 1978; the new [communist rulers](/wiki/Khalq "Khalq") were Ghilji.[[226]](#cite_note-245) Tribal allegiances are stronger among the Ghilji, while governance of the Durrani confederation is more to do with cross-tribal structures of land ownership.[[225]](#cite_note-USMA-244)

## Language

Main article: [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto")

See also: [Pashto alphabet](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet"), [Pashto dialects](/wiki/Pashto_dialects "Pashto dialects"), and [Pashto grammar](/wiki/Pashto_grammar "Pashto grammar")

Further information: [Pashto phonology](/wiki/Pashto_phonology "Pashto phonology"), [Wanetsi](/wiki/Wanetsi "Wanetsi"), [Languages of Afghanistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Afghanistan "Languages of Afghanistan"), and [Languages of Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Map_of_Pashto-speaking_areas_in_Afghanistan_and_Pakistan.svg/330px-Map_of_Pashto-speaking_areas_in_Afghanistan_and_Pakistan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Pashto-speaking_areas_in_Afghanistan_and_Pakistan.svg)

Map of [Pashto-speaking](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") areas in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")

[Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") is the [mother tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language") of most Pashtuns.[[227]](#cite_note-T&F-246)[[228]](#cite_note-Henderson-247)[[229]](#cite_note-Henderson_1983-248) It is one of the two [national languages](/wiki/National_language "National language") of [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[230]](#cite_note-Pashto-language-249)[[231]](#cite_note-socioling-250) In Pakistan, although being the second-largest language being spoken,[[232]](#cite_note-251) it is often neglected officially in the education system.[[233]](#cite_note-Sociolinguistic_Survey_of_Northern_Pakistan-252)[[234]](#cite_note-253)[[235]](#cite_note-254)[[236]](#cite_note-255)[[237]](#cite_note-256)[[238]](#cite_note-257) This has been criticized as adversely impacting the economic advancement of Pashtuns,[[239]](#cite_note-258)[[240]](#cite_note-259) as students do not have the ability to comprehend what is being taught in other languages fully.[[241]](#cite_note-260) Robert Nichols remarks:[[217]](#cite_note-Brill-236)

> The politics of writing Pashto language textbooks in a nationalist environment promoting integration through Islam and Urdu had unique effects. There was no lesson on any twentieth century Pakhtun, especially Abdul Ghaffar Khan, the anti-British, pro-Pakhtun nationalist. There was no lesson on the Pashtun state-builders in nineteenth and twentieth century Afghanistan. There was little or no sampling of original Pashto language religious or historical material.

— Language Policy and Language Conflict in Afghanistan and Its Neighbors, Chapter 8, page 278

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Pashto-speakers_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg/330px-Pashto-speakers_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pashto-speakers_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg)

The map of Pashto speaking areas of Pakistan

Pashto is categorized as an [Eastern Iranian](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") language,[[242]](#cite_note-Iranica-Pashto-261) but a remarkably large number of words are unique to Pashto.[[243]](#cite_note-BensonKosonen20132-262)[[244]](#cite_note-263) [Pashto morphology](/wiki/Pashto_grammar "Pashto grammar") in relation to verbs is complex compared to other Iranian languages.[[245]](#cite_note-264) In this respect [MacKenzie](/wiki/David_Neil_MacKenzie "David Neil MacKenzie") states:[[246]](#cite_note-265)

> If we compare the archaic structure of Pashto with the much simplified morphology of Persian, the leading modern Iranian language, we see that it stands to its 'second cousin' and neighbour in something like the same relationship as Icelandic does to English.

— David Neil MacKenzie

Pashto has a large number of [dialects](/wiki/Pashto_dialects "Pashto dialects"): generally divided into [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Pashto "Northern Pashto"), [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Pashto "Southern Pashto") and [Central](/wiki/Central_Pashto "Central Pashto") groups;[[247]](#cite_note-266) and also [Tarino or Waṇetsi](/wiki/Wanetsi "Wanetsi") as distinct group.[[248]](#cite_note-267)[[249]](#cite_note-268) As Elfenbein notes: "Dialect differences lie primarily in [phonology](/wiki/Pashto_phonology "Pashto phonology") and lexicon: the morphology and syntax are, again with the exception of [Wanetsi](/wiki/Wanetsi "Wanetsi"), quite remarkably uniform".[[250]](#cite_note-269) Ibrahim Khan provides the following classification on the letter ښ: the Northern Western dialect (e.g. spoken by the [Ghilzai](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji")) having the phonetic value /ç+/, the North Eastern (spoken by the [Yusafzais](/wiki/Yusufzai "Yusufzai") etc.) having the sound /x/, the South Western (spoken by the [Abdalis](/wiki/Durrani "Durrani") etc.) having /ʂ/ and the South Eastern (spoken by the [Kakars](/wiki/Kakar "Kakar") etc.) having /ʃ/.[[251]](#cite_note-Khan2021-270) He illustrates that the Central dialects, which are spoken by the [Karlāṇ tribes](/wiki/Karlani "Karlani"), can also be divided on the North /x/ and South /ʃ/ distinction but provides that in addition these Central dialects have had a vowel shift which makes them distinct: for instance /ɑ/ represented by [aleph](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet") the non-Central dialects becoming /ɔː/ in [Banisi](/wiki/Banuchi "Banuchi") dialect.[[251]](#cite_note-Khan2021-270)

The first Pashto alphabet was developed by [Pir Roshan](/wiki/Pir_Roshan "Pir Roshan") in the 16th century.[[252]](#cite_note-271) In 1958, a meeting of Pashtun scholars and writers from both Afghanistan and Pakistan, held in Kabul, standardized the present [Pashto alphabet](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet").[[253]](#cite_note-MacKenzie_1959_231–235-272)

## Culture

Main article: [Pashtun culture](/wiki/Pashtun_culture "Pashtun culture")

Further information: [Culture of Afghanistan](/wiki/Culture_of_Afghanistan "Culture of Afghanistan") and [Culture of Pakistan](/wiki/Culture_of_Pakistan "Culture of Pakistan")

See also: [Pashtun cuisine](/wiki/Pashtun_cuisine "Pashtun cuisine") and [Pashtun clothing](/wiki/Pashtun_clothing "Pashtun clothing")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Pashtuns.jpg/250px-Pashtuns.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pashtuns.jpg)

Local clothes used by Pashtun children

Pashtun culture is based on Pashtunwali, [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and the understanding of Pashto language. The Kabul dialect is used to standardize the present [Pashto alphabet](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet").[[253]](#cite_note-MacKenzie_1959_231–235-272) Poetry is also an important part of Pashtun culture and it has been for centuries.[[254]](#cite_note-273) Pre-Islamic traditions, dating back to [Alexander](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great")'s defeat of the [Persian Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") in 330 BC, possibly survived in the form of [traditional dances](/wiki/Attan "Attan"), while literary styles and music reflect influence from the [Persian tradition](/wiki/Culture_of_Iran "Culture of Iran") and regional [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") fused with localized variants and interpretation. Like other [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), Pashtuns celebrate [Islamic holidays](/wiki/Islamic_holidays "Islamic holidays"). Contrary to the Pashtuns living in Pakistan, [Nowruz in Afghanistan](/wiki/Nowruz_in_Afghanistan "Nowruz in Afghanistan") is celebrated as the Afghan New Year by all Afghan ethnicities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Jirga

Main article: [Jirga](/wiki/Jirga "Jirga")
