Another prominent Pashtun institution is the [*lóya jirgá*](/wiki/Loya_jirga "Loya jirga") ([Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): لويه جرګه) or 'grand council' of elected [elders](/wiki/Elder_(administrative_title) "Elder (administrative title)").[[255]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-274) Most decisions in tribal life are made by members of the [*jirgá*](/wiki/Jirga "Jirga") ([Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): جرګه), which has been the main institution of authority that the largely egalitarian Pashtuns willingly acknowledge as a viable governing body.[[256]](#cite_note-HRW-275)

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in Afghanistan](/wiki/Religion_in_Afghanistan "Religion in Afghanistan") and [Religion in Pakistan](/wiki/Religion_in_Pakistan "Religion in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Mosque_in_Kandahar-2011.jpg/250px-Mosque_in_Kandahar-2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosque_in_Kandahar-2011.jpg)

The [tomb of Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Tomb_of_Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Tomb of Ahmad Shah Durrani") in [Kandahar City](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar"), which also serves as the [Congregational Mosque](/wiki/Congregational_Mosque "Congregational Mosque") and contains the [sacred cloak](/wiki/Shrine_of_the_Cloak "Shrine of the Cloak") that the [Islamic prophet](/wiki/Islamic_prophet "Islamic prophet") [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") wore.

Before Islam there were various different beliefs which were practised by Pashtuns such as [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"),[[257]](#cite_note-276) [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism").[[258]](#cite_note-ArnoldNettl2000-277)

The overwhelming majority of Pashtuns adhere to [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") and belong to the [Hanafi](/wiki/Hanafi "Hanafi") school of thought. Small [Shia](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") communities exist in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Paktia. The Shias belong to the [Turi](/wiki/Turi_(tribe) "Turi (tribe)") tribe while the [Bangash](/wiki/Bangash "Bangash") tribe is approximately 50% Shia and the rest Sunni, who are mainly found in and around [Parachinar](/wiki/Parachinar "Parachinar"), [Kurram](/wiki/Kurram_District "Kurram District"), [Hangu](/wiki/Hangu_District,_Pakistan "Hangu District, Pakistan"), [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat "Kohat") and [Orakzai](/wiki/Orakzai_District "Orakzai District").[[259]](#cite_note-278)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Afghan_men_praying_in_Kunar-2009.jpg/250px-Afghan_men_praying_in_Kunar-2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_men_praying_in_Kunar-2009.jpg)

Men doing Islamic [salat](/wiki/Salat "Salat") (praying) outside in the open in the [Kunar Province](/wiki/Kunar_Province "Kunar Province") of Afghanistan

A legacy of [Sufi](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") activity may be found in some Pashtun regions, especially in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, as evident in songs and dances. Many Pashtuns are prominent [Ulema](/wiki/Ulema "Ulema"), Islamic scholars, such as Maulana Aazam an author of more than five hundred books including Tafasee of the Quran as Naqeeb Ut Tafaseer, Tafseer Ul Aazamain, Tafseer e Naqeebi and Noor Ut Tafaseer etc., as well as [Muhammad Muhsin Khan](/wiki/Muhammad_Muhsin_Khan "Muhammad Muhsin Khan") who has helped translate the [Noble Quran](/wiki/Noble_Qur%27an_(Hilali-Khan) "Noble Qur'an (Hilali-Khan)"), [Sahih Al-Bukhari](/wiki/Sahih_Bukhari "Sahih Bukhari") and many other books to the English language.[[260]](#cite_note-Noble_Quran-279) Many Pashtuns want to reclaim their identity from being lumped in with the Taliban and international terrorism, which is not directly linked with Pashtun culture and history.[[261]](#cite_note-280)

Little information is available on non-Muslim as there is limited data regarding [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") groups and minorities, especially since many of the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") and [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") Pashtuns migrated from Khyber Pakhtunkhwa after the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") and later, after the [rise of the Taliban](/wiki/Battle_of_Kabul_(1992%E2%80%931996) "Battle of Kabul (1992–1996)").[[262]](#cite_note-Tariq_Ali-281)[[263]](#cite_note-John_Trimbur-282)

There are also Hindus who claim to be Pashtun, sometimes known as the Sheen Khalai (though their ethnic claim is disputed by other Pashtuns), who have moved predominantly to India.[[264]](#cite_note-283)[[265]](#cite_note-284) A small Hindu community, known as the *[Sheen Khalai](/wiki/Sheen_Khalai "Sheen Khalai")* meaning 'blue skinned' (referring to the color of Pashtun women's facial [tattoos](/wiki/Tattoo "Tattoo")), migrated to Unniara, [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"), India after [partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India").[[266]](#cite_note-Haider2018-285) Prior to 1947, the community resided in the [Quetta](/wiki/Quetta "Quetta"), [Loralai](/wiki/Loralai "Loralai") and Maikhter regions of the British Indian province of [Baluchistan](/wiki/Baluchistan_(Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province) "Baluchistan (Chief Commissioner's Province)").[[267]](#cite_note-thebetterindia-286)[[266]](#cite_note-Haider2018-285)[[268]](#cite_note-hind-287) They are mainly claim to be members of the Pashtun [Kakar](/wiki/Kakar "Kakar") tribe. Today, they continue to speak Pashto and celebrate Pashtun culture through the [Attan](/wiki/Attan "Attan") dance.[[267]](#cite_note-thebetterindia-286)[[266]](#cite_note-Haider2018-285)

There is also a minority of Pashto speaking Sikhs in [Tirah](/wiki/Tirah "Tirah"), [Orakzai](/wiki/Orakzai_District "Orakzai District"), [Kurram](/wiki/Kurram_District "Kurram District"), [Malakand](/wiki/Malakand_District "Malakand District"), and [Swat](/wiki/Swat_District "Swat District"). Due to the ongoing [insurgency in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Insurgency in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), some Pashto speaking Sikhs were [internally displaced](/wiki/Internally_displaced_person "Internally displaced person") from their ancestral villages to settle in cities like [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") and [Nankana Sahib](/wiki/Nankana_Sahib "Nankana Sahib").[[269]](#cite_note-Eusufzye2018-288)[[270]](#cite_note-Sikhs-289)[[271]](#cite_note-Sikh_Pashtuns-290)

### Pashto literature and poetry

Main article: [Pashto literature and poetry](/wiki/Pashto_literature_and_poetry "Pashto literature and poetry")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Mahmud_Tarzi_in_1920-cropped.jpg/250px-Mahmud_Tarzi_in_1920-cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahmud_Tarzi_in_1920-cropped.jpg)

[Mahmud Tarzi](/wiki/Mahmud_Tarzi "Mahmud Tarzi"), son of [Ghulam Muhammad Tarzi](/wiki/Ghulam_Muhammad_Tarzi "Ghulam Muhammad Tarzi"), became the pioneer of [Afghan journalism](/wiki/Media_of_Afghanistan "Media of Afghanistan") for publishing the first newspaper [Seraj al Akhbar](/wiki/Seraj_al_Akhbar "Seraj al Akhbar").[[272]](#cite_note-291)

The majority of Pashtuns use [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") as their [native tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language"), belonging to the [Iranian](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages") [language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family"),[[273]](#cite_note-Iranica-292) and spoken by up to 60 million people.[[274]](#cite_note-Penzl-293)[[275]](#cite_note-Omniglot-294) It is written in the [Pashto-Arabic script](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet") and is divided into two main dialects, the southern "Pashto" and the northern "Pukhto". The language has ancient origins and bears similarities to [extinct languages](/wiki/Extinct_language "Extinct language") such as [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan_language "Avestan language") and [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactrian_language "Bactrian language").[[276]](#cite_note-Britannica_Avestan-295) Its closest modern relatives may include [Pamir languages](/wiki/Pamir_languages "Pamir languages"), such as [Shughni](/wiki/Shughni_language "Shughni language") and [Wakhi](/wiki/Wakhi_language "Wakhi language"), and [Ossetic](/wiki/Ossetic_language "Ossetic language").[[277]](#cite_note-296) Pashto may have ancient legacy of borrowing vocabulary from neighbouring languages including such as [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit"). Modern borrowings come primarily from the English language.[[278]](#cite_note-Pashto_Dictionary-297)

The earliest describes Sheikh Mali's conquest of [Swat](/wiki/Swat_(princely_state) "Swat (princely state)").[[279]](#cite_note-UCLA-298) [Pir Roshan](/wiki/Pir_Roshan "Pir Roshan") is believed to have written a number of Pashto books while fighting with the Mughals. Pashtun scholars such as [Abdul Hai Habibi](/wiki/Abdul_Hai_Habibi "Abdul Hai Habibi") and others believe that the earliest Pashto work dates back to [Amir Kror Suri](/wiki/Amir_Kror_Suri "Amir Kror Suri"), and they use the writings found in [Pata Khazana](/wiki/Pata_Khazana "Pata Khazana") as proof. Amir Kror Suri, son of *Amir Polad Suri*, was an 8th-century [folk hero](/wiki/Folk_hero "Folk hero") and king from the [Ghor](/wiki/Ghor_Province "Ghor Province") region in Afghanistan.[[280]](#cite_note-299)[[281]](#cite_note-300) However, this is disputed by several European experts due to lack of strong evidence.

The advent of poetry helped transition Pashto to the modern period. Pashto literature gained significant prominence in the 20th century, with poetry by [Ameer Hamza Shinwari](/wiki/Ameer_Hamza_Shinwari "Ameer Hamza Shinwari") who developed *Pashto Ghazals*.[[282]](#cite_note-Shinwari_Baba-301) In 1919, during the expanding of mass media, [Mahmud Tarzi](/wiki/Mahmud_Tarzi "Mahmud Tarzi") published Seraj-al-Akhbar, which became the first Pashto newspaper in Afghanistan. In 1977, [Khan Roshan Khan](/wiki/Khan_Roshan_Khan "Khan Roshan Khan") wrote *Tawarikh-e-Hafiz Rehmatkhani* which contains the family trees and Pashtun tribal names. Some notable poets include [Malak Ahmad Khan Yusufzai](/wiki/Malak_Ahmad_Khan_Yusufzai "Malak Ahmad Khan Yusufzai") [Abdul Ghani Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Ghani_Khan "Abdul Ghani Khan"), [Afzal Khan Khattak](/wiki/Afzal_Khan_Khattak "Afzal Khan Khattak"), [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani"), [Gaju Khan](/wiki/Gaju_Khan "Gaju Khan") [Kalu Khan Yousafzai](/wiki/Kalu_Khan_Yousafzai "Kalu Khan Yousafzai") [Ajmal Khattak](/wiki/Ajmal_Khattak "Ajmal Khattak"), [Ghulam Muhammad Tarzi](/wiki/Ghulam_Muhammad_Tarzi "Ghulam Muhammad Tarzi"), [Hamza Shinwari](/wiki/Hamza_Shinwari "Hamza Shinwari"), [Hanif Baktash](/wiki/Hanif_Baktash "Hanif Baktash"), [Khushal Khan Khattak](/wiki/Khushal_Khan_Khattak "Khushal Khan Khattak"), [Nazo Tokhi](/wiki/Nazo_Tokhi "Nazo Tokhi"), [Pareshan Khattak](/wiki/Pareshan_Khattak "Pareshan Khattak"), [Rahman Baba](/wiki/Rahman_Baba "Rahman Baba"), [Shuja Shah Durrani](/wiki/Shuja_Shah_Durrani "Shuja Shah Durrani"), and [Timur Shah Durrani](/wiki/Timur_Shah_Durrani "Timur Shah Durrani").[[283]](#cite_note-302)[[284]](#cite_note-Rahman_Baba-303)

### Media and arts

Further information: [Pashto media](/wiki/Pashto_media "Pashto media"), [Pashto music](/wiki/Pashto_music "Pashto music"), and [Pashto cinema](/wiki/Pashto_cinema "Pashto cinema")

[Pashto media](/wiki/Pashto_media "Pashto media") has expanded in the last decade, with a number of [Pashto TV channels](/wiki/List_of_Pashto-language_television_channels "List of Pashto-language television channels") becoming available. Two of the popular ones are the Pakistan-based [AVT Khyber](/wiki/AVT_Khyber "AVT Khyber") and Pashto One. Pashtuns around the world, particularly those in Arab countries, watch these for entertainment purposes and to get latest news about their native areas.[[285]](#cite_note-ATVKhyber-304) Others are Afghanistan-based [Shamshad TV](/wiki/Shamshad_TV "Shamshad TV"), [Radio Television Afghanistan](/wiki/Radio_Television_Afghanistan "Radio Television Afghanistan"), [TOLOnews](/wiki/TOLOnews "TOLOnews") and [Lemar TV](/wiki/Lemar_TV "Lemar TV"), which has a special children's show called *[Baghch-e-Simsim](/wiki/Baghch-e-Simsim "Baghch-e-Simsim")*. International news sources that provide Pashto programs include [BBC Pashto](/wiki/BBC_Pashto "BBC Pashto") and [Voice of America](/wiki/Voice_of_America#Languages "Voice of America").

Producers based in [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") have created [Pashto-language films](/wiki/List_of_Pashto-language_films "List of Pashto-language films") since the 1970s.

Pashtun performers remain avid participants in various physical forms of expression including dance, sword fighting, and other physical feats. Perhaps the most common form of artistic expression can be seen in the various forms of Pashtun dances. One of the most prominent dances is *[Attan](/wiki/Attan "Attan")*, which has ancient roots. A rigorous exercise, Attan is performed as musicians play various native instruments including the *[dhol](/wiki/Dhol "Dhol")* (drums), *[tablas](/wiki/Tabla "Tabla")* (percussions), *[rubab](/wiki/Rubab_(instrument) "Rubab (instrument)")* (a [bowed](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") [string instrument](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument")), and *toola* (wooden flute). With a rapid circular motion, dancers perform until no one is left dancing, similar to [Sufi](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") [whirling dervishes](/wiki/Mevlevi "Mevlevi"). Numerous other dances are affiliated with various tribes notably from Pakistan including the *Khattak Wal Atanrh* (eponymously named after the [Khattak](/wiki/Khattak "Khattak") tribe), *Mahsood Wal Atanrh* (which, in modern times, involves the juggling of loaded rifles), and *Waziro Atanrh* among others. A sub-type of the *Khattak Wal Atanrh* known as the *Braghoni* involves the use of up to three swords and requires great skill. Young women and girls often entertain at weddings with the *Tumbal* ([Dayereh](/wiki/Dayereh "Dayereh")) which is an instrument.[[286]](#cite_note-305)

### Sports

Further information: [Sport in Pakistan](/wiki/Sport_in_Pakistan "Sport in Pakistan") and [Sport in Afghanistan](/wiki/Sport_in_Afghanistan "Sport in Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Shahid_Afridi_2010-cropped.jpg/250px-Shahid_Afridi_2010-cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shahid_Afridi_2010-cropped.jpg)

[Shahid Afridi](/wiki/Shahid_Afridi "Shahid Afridi"), former captain of the [Pakistan national cricket team](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team")

Both the [Pakistan national cricket team](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team") and the [Afghanistan national cricket team](/wiki/Afghanistan_national_cricket_team "Afghanistan national cricket team") have Pashtun players.[[287]](#cite_note-EWC-306) One of the most popular sports among Pashtuns is [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), which was introduced to South Asia during the early 18th century with the arrival of the British. Many Pashtuns have become prominent international [cricketers](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), including [Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan "Imran Khan"), [Shahid Afridi](/wiki/Shahid_Afridi "Shahid Afridi"), [Majid Khan](/wiki/Majid_Khan_(cricketer,_born_1946) "Majid Khan (cricketer, born 1946)"), [Misbah-ul-Haq](/wiki/Misbah-ul-Haq "Misbah-ul-Haq"), [Younis Khan](/wiki/Younis_Khan "Younis Khan"),[[288]](#cite_note-307) [Umar Gul](/wiki/Umar_Gul "Umar Gul"),[[289]](#cite_note-308) [Junaid Khan](/wiki/Junaid_Khan_(cricketer) "Junaid Khan (cricketer)"),[[290]](#cite_note-309) [Fakhar Zaman](/wiki/Fakhar_Zaman_(cricketer) "Fakhar Zaman (cricketer)"),[[291]](#cite_note-310) [Mohammad Rizwan](/wiki/Mohammad_Rizwan_(cricketer) "Mohammad Rizwan (cricketer)"),[[292]](#cite_note-311) [Usman Shinwari](/wiki/Usman_Shinwari "Usman Shinwari"), [Naseem Shah](/wiki/Naseem_Shah_(cricketer) "Naseem Shah (cricketer)"), [Shaheen Afridi](/wiki/Shaheen_Afridi "Shaheen Afridi"), [Iftikhar Ahmed](/wiki/Iftikhar_Ahmed_(cricketer) "Iftikhar Ahmed (cricketer)"), [Mohammad Wasim](/wiki/Mohammad_Wasim_(cricketer,_born_2001) "Mohammad Wasim (cricketer, born 2001)") and [Yasir Shah](/wiki/Yasir_Shah "Yasir Shah").[[293]](#cite_note-312) Australian cricketer [Fawad Ahmed](/wiki/Fawad_Ahmed "Fawad Ahmed") is of Pakistani Pashtun origin who has played for the Australian national team.[[294]](#cite_note-313)

[Makha](/wiki/Makha "Makha") is a traditional archery sport in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, played with a long arrow (*gheshai*) having a saucer shaped metallic plate at its distal end, and a long bow.[[295]](#cite_note-314) In Afghanistan, some Pashtuns still participate in the ancient sport of [buzkashi](/wiki/Buzkashi "Buzkashi") in which horse riders attempt to place a goat or calf carcass in a goal circle.[[296]](#cite_note-315)[[297]](#cite_note-316)[[298]](#cite_note-317)

### Women

Pashtun women are known to be modest and honourable because of their modest dressing.[[299]](#cite_note-318)[[300]](#cite_note-319) The lives of Pashtun women vary from those who reside in the ultra-conservative rural areas to those found in urban centres.[[301]](#cite_note-Women's_Rights-320) At the village level, the female village leader is called "qaryadar". Her duties may include witnessing women's ceremonies, mobilizing women to practice religious festivals, preparing the female dead for burial, and performing services for deceased women. She also arranges marriages for her own family and arbitrates conflicts for men and women.[[302]](#cite_note-Harvard-321) Though many Pashtun women remain tribal and illiterate, some have completed universities and joined the regular employment world.[[301]](#cite_note-Women's_Rights-320)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/08/Shinz%C5%8D_Abe_and_Malala_Yousafzai_%281%29_Cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinz%C5%8D_Abe_and_Malala_Yousafzai_(1)_Cropped.jpg)

[Malala Yousafzai](/wiki/Malala_Yousafzai "Malala Yousafzai"), Pakistani activist for female education and the 2014 [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") laureate

The decades of war and the rise of the Taliban [caused considerable hardship](/wiki/Taliban_treatment_of_women "Taliban treatment of women") among Pashtun women, as many of their rights have been restricted by a rigid interpretation of [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia"). The difficult lives of Afghan female refugees gained considerable notoriety with the iconic image *[Afghan Girl](/wiki/Afghan_Girl "Afghan Girl")* (Sharbat Gula) depicted on the June 1985 cover of *[National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic_Magazine "National Geographic Magazine")* magazine.[[303]](#cite_note-Sharbat_Gula-322)

Modern social reform for Pashtun women began in the early 20th century, when Queen [Soraya Tarzi](/wiki/Soraya_Tarzi "Soraya Tarzi") of Afghanistan made rapid reforms to improve women's lives and their position in the family. She was the only woman to appear on the list of rulers in Afghanistan. Credited with having been one of the first and most powerful Afghan and Muslim female activists. Her advocacy of social reforms for women led to a protest and contributed to the ultimate demise of [King Amanullah](/wiki/Amanullah_Khan "Amanullah Khan")'s reign in 1929.[[304]](#cite_note-323) [Civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") remained an important issue during the 1970s, as feminist leader [Meena Keshwar Kamal](/wiki/Meena_Keshwar_Kamal "Meena Keshwar Kamal") campaigned for [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights_in_Afghanistan "Women's rights in Afghanistan") and founded the [Revolutionary Association of the Women of Afghanistan](/wiki/Revolutionary_Association_of_the_Women_of_Afghanistan "Revolutionary Association of the Women of Afghanistan") (RAWA) in the 1977.[[305]](#cite_note-RAWA-324)

Pashtun women these days vary from the traditional housewives who live in seclusion to urban workers, some of whom seek or have attained parity with men.[[301]](#cite_note-Women's_Rights-320) But due to numerous social hurdles, the literacy rate remains considerably lower for them than for males.[[306]](#cite_note-Afghan_women_literacy-325) Abuse against women is present and increasingly being challenged by women's rights organizations which find themselves struggling with conservative religious groups as well as government officials in both Pakistan and Afghanistan. According to a 1992 book, "a powerful ethic of forbearance severely limits the ability of traditional Pashtun women to mitigate the suffering they acknowledge in their lives."[[307]](#cite_note-Paxtun_Women-326)

Further challenging the status quo, [Vida Samadzai](/wiki/Vida_Samadzai "Vida Samadzai") was selected as Miss Afghanistan in 2003, a feat that was received with a mixture of support from those who back the individual rights of women and those who view such displays as anti-traditionalist and un-Islamic. Some have attained political office in Afghanistan and Pakistan.[[308]](#cite_note-327) A number of Pashtun women are found as TV hosts, journalists and actors.[[64]](#cite_note-Khyber-73) In 1942, [Madhubala](/wiki/Madhubala "Madhubala") (Mumtaz Jehan), the [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe") of India, entered the [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") film industry.[[187]](#cite_note-Devasher2022-206) Bollywood blockbusters of the 1970s and 1980s starred [Parveen Babi](/wiki/Parveen_Babi "Parveen Babi"), who hailed from the lineage of Gujarat's historical Pathan community: the royal [Babi Dynasty](/wiki/Babi_Dynasty "Babi Dynasty").[[309]](#cite_note-328) Other Indian actresses and models, such as [Zarine Khan](/wiki/Zarine_Khan "Zarine Khan"), continue to work in the industry.[[188]](#cite_note-pathan-207) During the 1980s many Pashtun women served in the ranks of the [Afghan communist regime's Military](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of Afghanistan"). [Khatol Mohammadzai](/wiki/Khatol_Mohammadzai "Khatol Mohammadzai") served paratrooper during the [Afghan Civil War](/wiki/Afghan_Civil_War_(1989%E2%80%931992) "Afghan Civil War (1989–1992)") and was later promoted to [brigadier general](/wiki/Brigadier_general "Brigadier general") in the [Afghan Army](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of Afghanistan").[[310]](#cite_note-329) [Nigar Johar](/wiki/Nigar_Johar "Nigar Johar") is a [three-star general](/wiki/Three-star_rank "Three-star rank") in the Pakistan Army, another Pashtun female became a fighter [pilot](/wiki/Aviator "Aviator") in the [Pakistan Air Force](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force").[[311]](#cite_note-Female_Pilots-330)
Pashtun women often have their legal rights curtailed in favour of their husbands or male relatives. For example, though women are officially [allowed to vote](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") in Pakistan, some have been kept away from [ballot boxes](/wiki/Ballot_box "Ballot box") by males.[[301]](#cite_note-Women's_Rights-320)

It would be pertinent to mention here that at the time of *Masudi* most of the Afghans were concentrated in Qandahar and adjacent areas and had not expanded to the north.
